title: EXPOSÉ XII MAXIMAL TORI, WEYL GROUP, CARTAN SUBGROUPS, REDUCTIVE CENTER OF SMOOTH AFFINE GROUP SCHEMES

pages.title: EXPOSÉ XII MAXIMAL TORI, WEYL GROUP, CARTAN SUBGROUPS, REDUCTIVE CENTER OF SMOOTH AFFINE GROUP SCHEMES

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XII
MAXIMAL TORI, WEYL GROUP, CARTAN SUBGROUPS, REDUCTIVE CENTER OF SMOOTH AFFINE GROUP SCHEMESby A. Grothendieck

Beginning with the present exposé, in contrast with the preceding ones, we will make use of the known results on the structure of smooth algebraic groups over an algebraically closed field \(k\), and above all of Borel's theory of affine algebraic groups, presented in the Chevalley Seminar 56/57: "Classification des groupes de Lie algébriques"1. Following the usage in the theory of algebraic groups, we refer to this Seminar by the acronym BIBLE. For the coming exposés, we will need in particular the results of BIBLE 4, 5, 6, 7 (the number after BIBLE refers to the number of the exposé). It seems, moreover, that the theory of schemes brings no notable simplification to Borel's theory as it is presented in BIBLE. This is why it did not seem useful to reproduce it in the present Seminar, our goal here being to deduce, from known results over algebraically closed fields, analogous results valid over an arbitrary base prescheme. (This will no longer be the case for Chevalley's structure theory of semisimple groups, which, it seems, is treated with advantage ab ovo over an arbitrary base prescheme).

In the oral exposés (which we have followed in Nos. 1 to 4), we restricted ourselves to affine group preschemes over S, relying in an essential way on the representability theorems of Exp. XI No. 4. In the present notes (cf. Nos. 6 to 8) we show quickly how one can eliminate the affineness hypotheses by a simpler method not using the most delicate results of Exposé XI. For other generalizations of the results contained in the present exposé, see also exposés XV and XVI. (It is clear that the content of exposés XI, XII, XV, XVI ought to be completely reworked).

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: This seminar has now been published, in a form revised by P. Cartier, as volume 3 of the Oeuvres de Chevalley (Springer, 2005).

blocks.html: 1. Maximal tori

Let G first be an algebraic group over an algebraically closed field \(k\). A maximal torus of G is an algebraic subgroup T of G which is a torus (which means here, \(k\) being algebraically closed, that it is isomorphic to a group of the form \(\mathbb{G}_m^r\)), and which is maximal for this property. Note that, \(k\) being perfect, \(G_{\mathrm{réd}}\) is a subgroup of G, smooth over \(k\), so it is essentially an algebraic group in the sense of BIBLE. Moreover, every reduced subgroup of G (such as a torus) is automatically a subgroup of \(G_{\mathrm{réd}}\), hence the maximal tori of G coincide with the maximal tori of \(G_{\mathrm{réd}}\). When G is affine, hence \(G_{\mathrm{réd}}\) affine, a fundamental theorem of Borel tells us that two maximal tori of G are conjugate by an element of \(\mathrm{G}(k) = G_{\mathrm{réd}}(k)\) (BIBLE 6, th. 4 c)), in particular they have the same dimension. We will call the common dimension of the maximal tori of G the reductive rank of G. Note moreover that the restriction that G be affine is unnecessary, as follows from a well-known theorem of Chevalley, asserting that every connected smooth algebraic group over \(k\) is an extension of an abelian variety by an affine group; cf. No. 6. In Nos. 1 to 4, we will most often confine ourselves to group preschemes affine over the base.

Let G be a smooth algebraic group over \(k\), and T a maximal torus of G; the centralizer \(\mathrm{C}(\mathrm{T})\) of T in G will be called the Cartan subgroup of G associated with T. For us it is a subgroup prescheme defined thanks to VIII 6.7, but one will note that G being smooth over \(k\), the same holds for the Cartan subgroup C, by virtue of XI 5.3, so in this case C is the unique subgroup prescheme of G smooth over \(k\) (i.e. an algebraic subgroup of G in the sense of BIBLE) such that \(\mathrm{C}(k)\) is the subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}(k)\) centralizing \(\mathrm{T}(k)\), i.e. it is essentially the centralizer in the sense of BIBLE. By the conjugation theorem already cited, the Cartan subgroups associated with the various maximal tori are conjugate to one another, hence have the same dimension. We will call their common dimension the nilpotent rank of G; it is equal to that of \(G_{\mathrm{réd}}\). Let \(\rho_{\mathrm r}(\mathrm{G})\) and \(\rho_{\mathrm n}(\mathrm{G})\) be the reductive and nilpotent ranks of G; then we have \[ \rho_{\mathrm r}(\mathrm{G}) \leqslant \rho_{\mathrm n}(\mathrm{G}), \] and the difference \[ \rho_{\mathrm u}(\mathrm{G}) = \rho_{\mathrm n}(\mathrm{G}) - \rho_{\mathrm r}(\mathrm{G}) = \dim \mathrm{C}/\mathrm{T} \] could be called, when G is affine, the unipotent rank of G. When G is smooth, affine and connected, then C is a nilpotent connected algebraic group (BIBLE 6, th. 6 a) and c)), hence (BIBLE 6, th. 2) isomorphic to the product \(\mathrm{C}_s \times \mathrm{C}_u\), where \(\mathrm{C}_s = \mathrm{T}\) is the maximal torus we started with (which is a fortiori a maximal torus in C), and where \(\mathrm{C}_u\) is a smooth unipotent subgroup, i.e. a successive extension of groups isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\) (BIBLE 6 th. 1 cor. 1 and 7, th. 4). We therefore also have, in this case: \[ \rho_{\mathrm u}(\mathrm{G}) = \dim \mathrm{C}_u. \]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.1

Besides the three notions of rank that we have just made precise for an affine algebraic group, there are two others which are useful, namely the semisimple rank \(\rho_{\mathrm s}(\mathrm{G})\), which is by definition the reductive rank of the quotient \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{R}\), where R is the radical of G, and finally the infinitesimal rank \(\rho_{\mathrm i}(\mathrm{G})\), which is by definition the nilpotent rank of the Lie algebra of G (which will be defined and studied in the next exposé). We will not use them in the present exposé. Let us only point out the inequalities: \[ \rho_{\mathrm s} \leqslant \rho_{\mathrm r} \leqslant \rho_{\mathrm n} \leqslant \rho_{\mathrm i}. \]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2

Let G be an algebraic group over the algebraically closed field \(k\), T an algebraic subgroup of G, \(k'\) an algebraically closed extension of \(k\), \(\mathrm{G}'\) and \(\mathrm{T}'\) the groups deduced from G, T by base extension. For T to be a maximal torus of G, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathrm{T}'\) be a maximal torus of \(\mathrm{G}'\).
This is an immediate consequence of the "principle of finite extension" EGA IV 9.1.1.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.3

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite type, T a subgroup prescheme of G. We say that T is a maximal torus of G if

  1. a)

    T is a torus (IX 1.3) and

  2. b)

    for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), denoting by \(\bar s\) the spectrum of an algebraic closure of \(\kappa(s)\), \(\mathrm{T}_{\bar s}\) is a maximal torus in \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar s}\).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 1.4

It follows from 1.2 that when S is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field we recover the usual definition, and that property 1.3 is stable under any base change. Note that by virtue of X 8.8 one may in definition 1.3 replace condition a) by the condition:

a') T is of finite presentation and flat over S.

One should be careful that a maximal torus in the sense of definition 1.3 is indeed maximal in the set of subtori of G (as follows at once from IX 2.9), but that the converse can be correct, the base S being say connected, only if G actually admits a maximal torus in the sense of 1.3, which is not the case in general (even if \(\mathrm{S} = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathbb{Z})\) and G is "semisimple"; see also 1.6). We will see however in XIV that the converse is true when S is artinian, or when S is a local scheme and G is "reductive": in this case, every torus of G is contained in a maximal torus.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.5

Let G be an algebraic group over a field \(k\). We call reductive rank (resp. nilpotent rank, resp. unipotent rank etc.) of G the reductive rank (resp. ...) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar k}\), where \(\bar k\) is an algebraic closure of \(k\).

One will note that by virtue of 1.2 and of the commutation of the formation of \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\) with base extension, the notions of rank introduced in 1.5 are invariant under extension of the base field; on the other hand, for \(k\) algebraically closed, they coincide with those introduced at the beginning of the present section.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.6

It is not difficult to construct an affine and smooth group scheme G over the spectrum S of a discrete valuation ring, whose generic fiber is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_m\), and whose special fiber is isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\). Such a G contains no torus except the trivial torus T (reduced to the unit subgroup), which is obviously not a maximal torus. More precisely, in the special fiber \(\mathrm{G}_0 = \mathbb{G}_{a,k}\), \(\mathrm{T}_0\) is indeed a maximal torus, but in the generic fiber \(\mathrm{G}_1 = \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm K}\), \(\mathrm{T}_1\) is no longer a maximal torus (\(k\) is the residue field, K the field of fractions of the valuation ring under consideration). One also sees from this example that the reductive rank of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) (\(s \in \mathrm{S}\)) is not a continuous function of \(s\). One has however the following results:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.7

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme affine and smooth over S. For every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), consider \(\rho_{\mathrm r}(s) = \rho_{\mathrm r}(\mathrm{G}_s)\) and \(\rho_{\mathrm n}(s) = \rho_{\mathrm n}(\mathrm{G}_s)\), the reductive and nilpotent ranks of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) (1.5). With these notations, we have the following:

a) The function \(\rho_{\mathrm r}\) on S is lower semicontinuous, the function \(\rho_{\mathrm n}\) on S is upper semicontinuous, hence the function \(\rho_{\mathrm u} = \rho_{\mathrm n} - \rho_{\mathrm r}\) is upper semicontinuous.

b) The following conditions (stable under arbitrary base change) are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    The function \(\rho_{\mathrm r}\) on S is locally constant.

  2. (ii)

    There exists locally, in the sense of the étale topology, a maximal torus in G.

  3. (ii bis)

    There exists locally, in the sense of the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology, a maximal torus in G.

c) Let \(\mathrm{T}_1, \mathrm{T}_2\) be two maximal tori in G (which implies that we are under the conditions considered in b)). Then \(\mathrm{T}_1, \mathrm{T}_2\) are conjugate locally in the sense of the étale topology, i.e. there exists a surjective étale morphism \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) such that the subgroups \((\mathrm{T}_1)_{\mathrm{S}'}\) and \((\mathrm{T}_2)_{\mathrm{S}'}\) of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) are conjugate by a section of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) over \(\mathrm{S}'\).

d) Under the conditions of b), i.e. when \(\rho_{\mathrm r}\) is locally constant, the same holds for \(\rho_{\mathrm n}\) (hence also for \(\rho_{\mathrm u} = \rho_{\mathrm n} - \rho_{\mathrm r}\)).

Proof. a) Let us note that for every morphism \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\), if \(\mathrm{G}' = \mathrm{G} \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm{S}'\), the functions \(\rho'_{\mathrm r}\) etc. on \(\mathrm{S}'\), defined in terms of \(\mathrm{G}'\) as \(\rho_{\mathrm r}\) etc. are in terms of G, are obtained simply by composing the latter with \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\). When \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) is faithfully flat quasi-compact, it follows that \(\rho'\) is continuous, resp. upper semicontinuous, resp. lower semicontinuous, if and only if \(\rho\) is, the Zariski topology of S being in fact a quotient of that of \(\mathrm{S}'\) (SGA 1 VIII 4.3). Consequently, the assertions of a) are local for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology. So let \(s \in \mathrm{S}\); we want to show that the set U of those \(t \in \mathrm{S}\) such that \(\rho_{\mathrm r}(t) \geqslant \rho_{\mathrm r}(s)\) (resp. \(\rho_{\mathrm n}(t) \leqslant \rho_{\mathrm n}(s)\)) is a neighborhood of \(s\). By virtue of the principle of finite extension, there exists a finite extension \(k\) of \(\kappa(s)\), such that \(\mathrm{G}_k\) admits a maximal torus. There then exists an open neighborhood U of \(s\), and a finite surjective locally free morphism \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{U}\), such that the fiber \(\mathrm{S}'_s\) is \(\kappa(s)\)-isomorphic to \(\mathrm{Spec}(k)\) (cf. EGA III 10.3.2, where the noetherian hypothesis is manifestly unnecessary). Since \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) is an open morphism (SGA 1 IV 6.6), we are reduced to the case where \(\mathrm{S}' = \mathrm{S}\), i.e. to the case where there exists a maximal torus \(\mathrm{T}_s\) in \(\mathrm{G}_s\). Moreover, thanks to XI 5.8 a), after possibly again replacing S by an \(\mathrm{S}'\) étale over S and equipped with a point \(s'\) above \(s\), we may assume that \(\mathrm{T}_s\) is the fiber at \(s\) of a subtorus T of G. Then for every \(t \in \mathrm{S}\), \(\rho_{\mathrm r}(t) = \rho_{\mathrm r}(\mathrm{G}_t) \geqslant \dim \mathrm{T}_t = \dim \mathrm{T}_s = \rho_{\mathrm r}(\mathrm{G}_s) = \rho_{\mathrm r}(s)\), which proves that \(\rho_{\mathrm r}\) is lower semicontinuous. On the other hand, by virtue of XI 5.3, the functor \[ \mathrm{C} = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T) \] is representable by \(\mathrm{C} = \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\)2, a closed subgroup prescheme of G smooth over S. Hence there exists a neighborhood U of \(s\) such that \(t \in \mathrm{U}\) implies \(\dim \mathrm{C}_t = \dim \mathrm{C}_s = \rho_{\mathrm n}(s)\). The upper semicontinuity of \(\rho_{\mathrm n}\) is then a consequence of the relation \[ \rho_{\mathrm n}(t) \leqslant \dim \mathrm{C}_t \qquad \text{for every} \quad t \in \mathrm{S}, \] which is itself contained in the following purely geometric lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.8

Let \(k\) be a field, G an affine and smooth algebraic group over \(k\), T a torus in G, C its centralizer; then we have \[ \rho_{\mathrm n}(\mathrm{G}) \leqslant \dim \mathrm{C}. \]

Indeed, we may assume \(k\) algebraically closed, so that T is contained in a maximal torus \(\mathrm{T}'\). Let \(\mathrm{C}'\) be the centralizer of the latter; then we have \(\mathrm{C}' \subset \mathrm{C}\), hence \(\dim \mathrm{C}' \leqslant \dim \mathrm{C}\). Q.E.D.

b) If \(\rho_{\mathrm r}\) is locally constant, then for every torus T of G, and every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), if \(\mathrm{T}_s\) is a maximal torus in \(\mathrm{G}_s\), there exists an open neighborhood U of \(s\) such that \(\mathrm{T}|_{\mathrm U}\) is a maximal torus in \(\mathrm{G}|_{\mathrm U}\). Using now the reasoning of a), one sees that (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii bis). On the other hand (ii bis) \(\Rightarrow\) (i), for when G admits a maximal torus T, then it is obvious that \(\rho_{\mathrm r}(s) = \dim \mathrm{T}_s\) is a locally constant function of \(s\), and we pointed out at the beginning of the proof of a) that the question of the continuity of \(\rho_{\mathrm r}\) was local for the faithfully flat topology. Hence (i) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (ii bis), and obviously (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii bis); it remains to prove the converse implication (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii). For this, let us introduce the functor \(F\) of XI 4.13, which is thus representable by a prescheme smooth and separated over S, and consider the subfunctor \(\mathscr{T}\) of \(F\), whose value for an \(\mathrm{S}'\) over S is the set of maximal tori in \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\). Using the preceding remark that, granting (i), a torus in \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) which is maximal in the fiber of a point \(s \in \mathrm{S}'\) is maximal in an open neighborhood of \(s\), one sees that \(\mathscr{T}\) is representable by an open subprescheme of \(F\), and consequently is smooth and separated over S. Since the structural morphism \(\mathscr{T} \to \mathrm{S}\) is obviously surjective, it therefore admits a section locally in the sense of the étale topology by virtue of XI 1.10, which proves (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii).
c) This is an immediate consequence of XI 5.4 bis, taking into account Borel's conjugation theorem recalled at the beginning of this section.

d) By the remark at the beginning of the proof in a), and taking b) into account, we may assume that there exists a maximal torus T in G. If C is its centralizer, then C is representable and is smooth over S by XI 5.3, hence the function \(s \mapsto \rho_{\mathrm n}(s) = \dim \mathrm{C}_s\) is indeed locally constant.
The proof of 1.7 is complete. We will refer to the conditions considered in 1.7 b) by saying that in this case, G is of locally constant reductive rank. Let us note:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.9

Let G be as in 1.7, and let \(s \in \mathrm{S}\) be such that \(\rho_{\mathrm u}(s) = 0\) i.e. \(\rho_{\mathrm r}(s) = \rho_{\mathrm n}(s)\), (i.e. the Cartan subgroups of \(\mathrm{G}_k\) are tori, where \(k\) is an algebraic closure of \(\kappa(s)\)). Then there exists an open neighborhood U of \(s\) on which \(\rho_{\mathrm r}\) and \(\rho_{\mathrm n}\) are constant, in particular for every \(t \in \mathrm{U}\), the unipotent rank \(\rho_{\mathrm u}(t)\) of \(\mathrm{G}_t\) is zero.
Indeed, this follows immediately from 1.7 a) and from the inequality \(\rho_{\mathrm r}(t) \leqslant \rho_{\mathrm n}(t)\) for every \(t\).
Let us also note that we have proved, at the same time as b), the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.10

Let G be as in 1.7, and suppose G is of locally constant reductive rank. Consider the functor \[ \mathscr{T} : (\mathrm{Sch})^{\circ} \longrightarrow (\mathrm{Ens}), \] such that for every \(\mathrm{S}'\) over S, we have \[ \mathscr{T}(\mathrm{S}') = \text{ set of maximal tori of } \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}. \] Then \(\mathscr{T}\) is representable by a prescheme smooth, separated and of finite type over S.

It remains to verify that \(\mathscr{T}\) is of finite type over S. The question being local for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology, we may assume that G admits a maximal torus T. By XI 5.3 bis and the bis version of 5.5, \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\) and \(\mathrm{G}/\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\) are representable by preschemes, and \(\mathscr{T}\) is isomorphic to \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\)4. The morphism \(\mathrm{G} \to \mathscr{T}\) defined by \(g \mapsto \mathrm{int}(g)(\mathrm T)\) being surjective, and G quasi-compact over S, the same holds for \(\mathscr{T}\), which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 1.11
  1. a)

    The prescheme \(\mathscr{T}\) of 1.10 will be called, as is fitting, the prescheme of maximal tori of G. We will see in No. 5 that it is in fact affine over S. This can be verified directly, when G is isomorphic to a closed subgroup prescheme of a prescheme of the form \(\mathrm{GL}(n)\), by using XI 4.6 and remarking that by the reasoning of the proof of 1.7 b), \(\mathscr{T}\) is identified with a subprescheme, both open and closed, of the prescheme \(F\) which represents the subgroups of multiplicative type of G.

  2. b)

    It is possible to give a proof of 1.7, hence of 1.9, not using the delicate results of XI, but only the easy results XI 3.12 and 6.2, by working only with multiplicative type groups finite over the base (morally, the groups \({}_n\mathrm{T}\) where T is a maximal torus), compare No. 7. The same remark holds for the proof of 1.10.
We end this section by giving examples where there exists a unique maximal torus.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.12

Let G be an S-group prescheme of multiplicative type and of finite type. Then G admits a unique maximal torus, and every torus in G is contained in this maximal torus.

The uniqueness obviously follows from this last assertion, which characterizes the maximal torus as the largest subtorus of G. From the uniqueness it follows that the question of existence is local for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology, which allows us to assume G diagonalizable, i.e. of the form \(\mathbf{D}_{\mathrm S}(\mathrm M)\), M a commutative group of finite type. Let \(\mathrm M_0\) be the quotient of M by its torsion subgroup; I claim that the torus \(\mathrm T = \mathbf{D}_{\mathrm S}(\mathrm M_0)\) in G is a maximal torus and a largest subtorus. Indeed, a subtorus \(\mathrm T'\) of G is locally diagonalizable for the fpqc topology, so to prove \(\mathrm T' \subset \mathrm T\), we may assume \(\mathrm T'\) diagonalizable, hence of the form \(\mathbf{D}_{\mathrm S}(\mathrm N)\), where N is a free quotient of M (VIII 1.4 and 3.2 b)), hence N is a quotient of \(\mathrm M_0\), hence \(\mathrm T' \subset \mathrm T\). Since the construction of T as \(\mathbf{D}_{\mathrm S}(\mathrm M_0)\) is compatible with any base extension, this shows at the same time that T is a maximal torus of G, and completes the proof. In the case where G is smooth over S, one can generalize 1.12:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.13

Let G be an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S. Suppose that G admits, locally for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology, a central maximal torus. Then G admits (globally) a unique maximal torus5, and it is the largest subtorus of G.

Here again, the uniqueness is trivial from the last assertion, and makes the existence a local question for fpqc, which allows us to assume that G admits a central maximal torus T. Let us show that every torus R of G is contained in T. This follows from the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.14

Let G be an S-group prescheme, of finite presentation over S, T a maximal torus of G, R a subtorus of G, and suppose that T and R commute. Then \(\mathrm R \subset \mathrm T\).

Indeed, since R and T commute, the morphism \(\mathrm R \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm T \to \mathrm G\) defined by \((r,t) \mapsto r \cdot t\) is a group homomorphism, hence by virtue of IX 6.8 it admits an image subgroup \(\mathrm T'\) in G, which is a multiplicative type group quotient of \(\mathrm R \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm T\), hence a torus, obviously containing T. Since T is a maximal torus, we have \(\mathrm T' = \mathrm T\) (1.4), hence \(\mathrm R \subset \mathrm T\), which proves 1.14 and hence 1.13. In particular, using 1.7 b):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.15

Let G be a commutative S-group prescheme, smooth and affine over S, of locally constant reductive rank. Then G admits a unique maximal torus, and the latter contains every subtorus of G.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.16

Let G be an S-group prescheme, smooth and affine over S. Suppose that for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), denoting by \(\bar s\) the spectrum of an algebraic closure \(k\) of \(\kappa(s)\), the geometric fiber \(\mathrm{G}_{\bar s}\) is a connected algebraic group, and nilpotent (in the sense of BIBLE, i.e. the group \(\mathrm{G}(k)\) of its points with values in \(k\) is nilpotent). Suppose moreover that the reductive rank of G is locally constant. Then G admits a unique maximal torus T; moreover T is central and it is the largest subtorus of G.

By virtue of 1.7 b), G admits locally for fpqc a maximal torus, and by virtue of 1.13 we are reduced to proving that a maximal torus of G is central. By virtue of IX 5.6 b), we are reduced to the case where S is the spectrum of a field, which we may assume algebraically closed. Then \(\mathrm T(k)\) is in the center of \(\mathrm G(k)\) by virtue of BIBLE 6 th. 2, which implies that T is in the center of G, for \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\) is a closed subscheme of G (VIII 6.6), which contains the points of \(\mathrm G(k)\), hence is identical to G by the Nullstellensatz (G being reduced).
Finally, for later reference, let us point out the following trivial proposition, (of which we have already made implicit use):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.17

Let \(\mathrm G \supset \mathrm H \supset \mathrm T\) be group preschemes of finite type over S. Equivalent conditions:

  1. (i)

    T is a maximal torus of G.

  2. (ii)

    T is a maximal torus of H, and for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), the reductive ranks are equal: \(\rho_{\mathrm r}(\mathrm{G}_s) = \rho_{\mathrm r}(\mathrm{H}_s)\).

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: modification made to introduce the prescheme \(\mathrm C = \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\) representing the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\).
N.D.E.: This is the functor of subgroups of multiplicative type of G.
N.D.E.: modification made to introduce the preschemes \(\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\) and \(\mathrm G/\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\).

blocks.html: 2. The Weyl group

Let \(k\) first be an algebraically closed field, and let G be an algebraic group over \(k\), smooth and affine over \(k\). If T is a maximal torus, C its centralizer and N its normalizer in G, then by virtue of XI 5.9 these are smooth closed subgroups of G, and C is an open subgroup of N, so that \(\mathrm W = \mathrm N/\mathrm C\) is a finite group étale over \(k\), hence determined by the group \(\mathrm W(k)\) of its points with values in \(k\), as \(\mathrm W = \mathrm W(k)_k\) (constant group defined by the ordinary finite group \(\mathrm W(k)\)). The finite group \(\mathrm W(k)\) will be called the geometric Weyl group (or simply Weyl group if no confusion is to be feared) of G relative to T. By virtue of Borel's conjugation theorem, the Weyl groups relative to the various maximal tori are isomorphic to one another, which is why one sometimes speaks of "the" Weyl group of G, without specifying a maximal torus. Since the formation of C, N, and N/C commutes with any base extension, one sees that if \(k'\) is an algebraically closed extension of \(k\), the geometric Weyl group of \(\mathrm G_{k'}\) relative to \(\mathrm T_{k'}\) is canonically isomorphic to that of G relative to T; consequently, "the" geometric Weyl group of G (which is, properly speaking, an isomorphism class of ordinary finite groups) coincides with that of \(\mathrm G'\).

This allows one, when G is a smooth affine algebraic group over an arbitrary field \(k\), to speak of the geometric Weyl group of G as being the isomorphism class of the geometric Weyl group of \(\mathrm G_{k'}\), where \(k'\) is an arbitrary algebraically closed extension of \(k\). When G admits the maximal torus T, then one can obviously form as above \(\mathrm C = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\), \(\mathrm N = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\), \(\mathrm W = \mathrm N/\mathrm C\), which is a finite group étale over \(k\), called the Weyl group of G relative to T; the geometric Weyl group is then nothing other than the class of the group of points of W with values in an arbitrary algebraically closed extension \(k'\) of \(k\). Here, knowledge of the geometric Weyl group \(\mathrm W(k')\) obviously no longer suffices in general to reconstruct the algebraic group W: one must in addition know the operations on \(\mathrm W(k')\) of the Galois group of the separable algebraic closure of \(k\) in \(k'\).

When finally G is a group prescheme over an arbitrary base S, G smooth and affine over S, and if T is a maximal torus of G, then XI 5.9 again allows us to form the group \[ \mathrm W(\mathrm T) = \mathrm N(\mathrm T)/\mathrm C(\mathrm T), \] which is an S-group prescheme étale, separated and quasi-finite over S. Its geometric fibers (relative to the algebraic closures of the residue fields \(\kappa(s)\), \(s \in \mathrm S\)) are the geometric Weyl groups of the fibers \(\mathrm G_s\). Consequently, XI 5.10 gives us information on the variation of these groups with \(s \in \mathrm S\). We can make this information more precise and generalize it in the following way:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth and affine over S. For every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), let \(w(s)\) be the geometric Weyl group of \(\mathrm G_s\), which is thus a class of finite groups up to isomorphism. In the set E of such classes, let us introduce the following preorder relation: \(w \leqslant w'\) if and only if \(w\) and \(w'\) are represented by finite groups W and \(\mathrm W'\), such that W is isomorphic to a quotient of a subgroup of \(\mathrm W'\). With this convention:

a) The function \(s \mapsto w(s)\) from S to E is lower semicontinuous.

b) Suppose that the reductive rank of G is locally constant. Then the following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    The function \(s \mapsto w(s)\) is locally constant.

  2. (i bis)

    The function \(s \mapsto \mathrm{card}\, w(s)\) is locally constant.

  3. (ii)

    There exists locally, in the sense of the étale topology (or only for the fpqc topology), a maximal torus T, such that \(\mathrm W(\mathrm T)\) is finite over its base.

  4. (ii bis)

    For every \(\mathrm S'\) over S, and every maximal torus T of \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm{S}'}\), the associated Weyl group \(\mathrm W(\mathrm T)\) is finite over \(\mathrm S'\).

Proof. a) Proceeding as in 1.7 a), we are reduced, in order to prove that for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\) there exists an open neighborhood U of \(s\) such that \(t \in \mathrm{U}\) implies \(w(t) \geqslant w(s)\), to the case where there exists a torus R in G such that \(\mathrm R_s\) is a maximal torus in \(\mathrm G_s\). Let \[ \mathrm W(\mathrm R) = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm R)/\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm R) \] as in XI 5.9; it is a group prescheme étale, separated, quasi-finite over S. For every \(t \in \mathrm{S}\), let \(w'(t) \in \mathrm E\) be its geometric fiber at \(t\). Since \(\mathrm R_s\) is a maximal torus in \(\mathrm G_s\), and the formation of Norm, Centr, Norm/Centr is compatible with any base extension, in particular passage to fibers, one sees that we have \[ w(s) = w'(s)\, ; \] I claim that moreover, for \(t\) near \(s\), we will have the inequalities \[ w(t) \geqslant w'(t) \qquad \text{and} \qquad w'(t) \geqslant w'(s), \] which will suffice to establish a). These two inequalities are contained in the two following lemmas:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.2

Let S be a prescheme, W an S-group prescheme which is étale, separated and quasi-finite over S. For every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), let \(f(s)\) be the class of the geometric fiber of W at \(s\), which is an element of the ordered set E of classes of finite groups up to isomorphism, introduced in 2.1. Then the function \(f : \mathrm S \to \mathrm E\) is lower semicontinuous. For it to be constant in a neighborhood of \(s\), it is necessary and sufficient that the same hold for the function \(s \mapsto \mathrm{card}\, f(s)\), and for this it is necessary and sufficient that W be finite over S above an open neighborhood U of \(s\).

This result is a refinement, in terms of groups, of the one invoked in the proof of XI 5.10; we confine ourselves to a sketch of the proof (which is of the most standard type). We are reduced as usual to the case S affine noetherian. One sees at once that the function \(f\) is constructible (EGA 0III 9.3.1 and 9.3.2, and the sorites of EGA IV 9) and by virtue of EGA 0III 9.3.4 we are reduced, for the semicontinuity, to proving that if \(t\) is a generization of \(s\), we have \(f(t) \geqslant f(s)\). This brings us, thanks to EGA II 7.1.7, to the case where S is the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, which we may assume complete with algebraically closed residue field. But then, \(s\) denoting the closed point of S, since W is étale and separated over S, it contains a subscheme \(\mathrm G'\), both open and closed, finite over S, such that \(\mathrm G'_s = \mathrm G_s\) (EGA II 6.2.6), and one sees at once that \(\mathrm G'\) is here a subgroup of G. Moreover \(\mathrm G'\), being finite étale over \(\mathrm S = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm V)\), V complete with algebraically closed residue field, is a constant group, hence of the form \(\mathrm A_{\mathrm S}\), where \(\mathrm A = \mathrm G'(\kappa(s)) = \mathrm G(\kappa(s))\) has class \(f(s)\). If B is the geometric fiber of G at the generic point \(t\) of S, we thus have a canonical monomorphism \(\mathrm A \to \mathrm B\), which proves \(f(s) \leqslant f(t)\). (N.B. this argument in fact proves the semicontinuity for an order relation on E finer than the one indicated in 2.1). The fact that \(f\) is continuous at \(s\) if and only if \(s \mapsto \mathrm{card}\, f(s)\) is, follows from the fact that for \(w, w' \in \mathrm E\), the relations \(w \leqslant w'\) and \(\mathrm{card}\, w = \mathrm{card}\, w'\) imply \(w = w'\). The fact that this condition is equivalent to the finiteness of W over a neighborhood of \(s\) is then independent of the group structure on W, and was pointed out after XI 5.10; moreover its proof is easily carried out by the preceding arguments, using the valuative criterion of properness EGA II 7.3.8.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.3

Let G be a smooth affine algebraic group over an algebraically closed field \(k\), \(\mathrm R \subset \mathrm T\) two subtori, \(\mathrm W(\mathrm R)\) and \(\mathrm W(\mathrm T)\) the two finite groups associated as in XI 5.9, quotients of the normalizer by the centralizer. Then \(\mathrm W(\mathrm R)\) is isomorphic to a quotient of a subgroup of \(\mathrm W(\mathrm T)\).

Indeed, consider the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \mathrm T \ar@{^{(}->}[r] & \mathrm C(\mathrm T) \ar@{^{(}->}[r] \ar@{^{(}->}[d] & \mathrm C(\mathrm R) \ar@{^{(}->}[d] \\ & \mathrm N(\mathrm T) \cap \mathrm N(\mathrm R) \ar@{^{(}->}[r] \ar@{^{(}->}[d] & \mathrm N(\mathrm R) \\ & \mathrm N(\mathrm T) & } \] then \((\mathrm N(\mathrm T) \cap \mathrm N(\mathrm R))/\mathrm C(\mathrm T)\) is a subgroup of \(\mathrm W(\mathrm T) = \mathrm N(\mathrm T)/\mathrm C(\mathrm T)\), and we have an obvious homomorphism: \[ (\mathrm N(\mathrm T) \cap \mathrm N(\mathrm R))/\mathrm C(\mathrm T) \longrightarrow \mathrm W(\mathrm R) = \mathrm N(\mathrm R)/\mathrm C(\mathrm R), \] and everything comes down to proving that the latter is surjective, hence that for every point \(g\) of \(\mathrm N(\mathrm R)\) with values in \(k\), there exists a point \(c\) of \(\mathrm C(\mathrm R)\) with values in \(k\) such that \(cg\) normalizes T, i.e. such that \[ \mathrm{int}(c)(\mathrm{int}(g)\mathrm T) = \mathrm T. \] Now for this, it suffices to note that \(\mathrm{int}(g)\mathrm T\) is a torus of \(\mathrm N(\mathrm R)\), hence of \(\mathrm C(\mathrm R)\) (which is an open subgroup of it). Then T and \(\mathrm{int}(g)\mathrm T\) are maximal tori of \(\mathrm C(\mathrm R)\), since they are maximal in G, and we conclude by Borel's conjugation theorem.
This proves 2.3 and thereby 2.1 a).

b) We have already pointed out that (i) and (i bis) are trivially equivalent; they imply (ii bis) by the converse to 2.2 or XI 5.10, as one prefers; (ii bis) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii) thanks to 1.7 b); finally, as one sees as in 1.7 a) that condition (i) is local for fpqc, this allows us to assume that G admits a maximal torus T such that \(\mathrm W(\mathrm T)\) is finite over S, and we conclude again by XI 5.10. This completes the proof of 2.1.
3. Cartan subgroups

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.1

Let G be a smooth group prescheme of finite type over the prescheme S. A Cartan subgroup of G is a subgroup prescheme C of G, smooth over S, such that for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), denoting by \(\bar s\) the spectrum of an algebraic closure of \(\kappa(s)\), \(\mathrm C_{\bar s}\) is a Cartan subgroup (cf. No. 1) of \(\mathrm G_{\bar s}\).

It is immediate that if C is a Cartan subgroup of G, then for every \(\mathrm S'\) over S, \(\mathrm C_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm{S}'}\). If S is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field, we immediately recover the notion recalled in No. 1. One checks at once that the property, for a subgroup prescheme C of G, of being a Cartan subgroup is of a local nature for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.2 6

Let G over S be as in 3.1, and suppose that G is affine over S, and of locally constant reductive rank. Then the map \[ \mathrm T \longmapsto \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T) \] induces a bijection from the set of maximal tori of G to the set of Cartan subgroups of G. If C corresponds to T, then T is the unique maximal torus of C.

If T is a maximal torus of G, the functor \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\) is representable, by virtue of XI 5.3 or 6.2, by a closed and smooth subprescheme of G, \(\mathrm C = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\), and it follows from the definitions that C is a Cartan subgroup of G. Moreover, T is obviously a maximal torus of C, and being central, it is the unique maximal torus of C (1.13). Hence the map \(\mathrm T \mapsto \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\) from the set of maximal tori to the set of Cartan subgroups is injective; it remains to prove that it is surjective. So let C be a Cartan subgroup of G; let us prove that it is of the form \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\), for T a maximal torus of G. For this it suffices to find a maximal torus T of C, for then T will be a maximal torus of G (since for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(\mathrm G_s\) and \(\mathrm C_s\) have the same reductive rank). By virtue of IX 5.6 b) T is in the center of C, hence \(\mathrm C \subset \mathrm C' = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\), and then C is a smooth subgroup of the smooth group \(\mathrm C'\) over S, coinciding with \(\mathrm C'\) fiber by fiber, whence \(\mathrm C = \mathrm C'\). Now since G is of locally constant reductive rank, the same holds for C, so C admits a maximal torus locally for the étale topology by virtue of 1.7 b), and since this torus is central by the preceding reasoning, it follows by 1.13 that C does admit a maximal torus, which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3

Let G be a group prescheme smooth and affine over S of locally constant reductive rank, and let \(\mathscr{C} : (\mathrm{Sch})^{\circ}_{/\mathrm S} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) be the functor defined by \[ \mathscr{C}(\mathrm S') = \text{ set of Cartan subgroups of } \mathrm G_{\mathrm{S}'}. \] Then the functor \(\mathscr{C}\) is isomorphic to the same functor \(\mathscr{T}\) of 1.10, hence is representable by the same prescheme smooth, separated and of finite type over S.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4

Under the conditions of 3.2, if \(\mathrm C = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\), we have \[ \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm C) = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T). \]

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 3.5

When G is not of locally constant reductive rank, it is nevertheless possible that G admits Cartan subgroups (for example if G has connected nilpotent fibers, G is a Cartan subgroup of itself, but is not necessarily of locally constant reductive rank, cf. 1.6). In XV, we develop the theory of Cartan subgroups without assuming G affine over S or of locally constant reductive rank, using the theory of regular elements of the next exposé. See also Nos. 6 and 7 for the elimination of the affineness hypothesis.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: The proof given here in fact only proves 3.2 for the closed Cartan subgroups of G. However, 7.1 a) establishes 3.2 in the form stated, and implies that the Cartan subgroups of G are closed.

blocks.html: 4. The reductive center

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.1

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group of finite presentation over S, with affine fibers, Z a subgroup prescheme of G. We say that Z is a reductive center of G if

  1. (i)

    Z is central, and of multiplicative type and

  2. (ii)

    for every base change \(\mathrm S' \to \mathrm S\), and every central homomorphism \(u : \mathrm H \to \mathrm G_{\mathrm{S}'}\), where H is a group of multiplicative type and of finite type over S, \(u\) factors through \(\mathrm Z_{\mathrm{S}'}\).

(For a variant of this notion when the fibers of G are not assumed affine, cf. 8.6.)

Let us note that such a Z is necessarily of finite type over S (since its fibers are), hence is uniquely determined as the largest central subgroup of multiplicative type of G. It is easy to give examples where G (smooth and affine over S) admits a largest central subgroup of multiplicative type Z, but where Z is not a reductive center (i.e. G does not admit a reductive center), cf. for example 1.6; it follows easily from IX 6.8, however, that a subgroup Z of G is a reductive center of G if and only if it is a largest central subgroup of multiplicative type, and keeps this property under any base change; see also 4.3 below.

It is obvious that if Z is the reductive center of G, then for every base change \(\mathrm S' \to \mathrm S\), \(\mathrm Z_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is the reductive center of \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm{S}'}\). From this, and from the uniqueness of the reductive center, it follows thanks to the theory of faithfully flat quasi-compact descent (SGA 1 VIII):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.2

Let G be an S-group prescheme of finite presentation over S and with affine fibers. If G admits a reductive center locally for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology on S, it admits a reductive center. For Z to be a reductive center of G, it is necessary and sufficient that it be one locally for the fpqc topology.
Let us also note:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.3

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme of finite presentation and with affine fibers, Z a subgroup prescheme. For Z to be a reductive center of G, it is necessary and sufficient that it be of multiplicative type, and that for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(\mathrm Z_s\) be a reductive center of \(\mathrm G_s\).

Indeed, it follows first from IX 5.6 b) that Z is then central. Since the conditions considered are stable under base change, it remains to prove that every central homomorphism \(u : \mathrm H \to \mathrm G\), with H of multiplicative type and of finite type, factors through Z. Now, Z being central, \(u\) and the canonical immersion \(v : \mathrm Z \to \mathrm G\) define a group homomorphism \[ w : \mathrm H \underset{\mathrm S}{\times} \mathrm Z \longrightarrow \mathrm G. \] By virtue of IX 6.8 the latter admits an image group K, which is a subgroup of multiplicative type of G, and everything comes down to proving that we have \(\mathrm K = \mathrm Z\). Now this is true fiber by fiber by the hypothesis on Z, and it now suffices to apply IX 5.1 bis, which completes the proof of 4.3.

One will note that in the criterion 4.3, the hypothesis that for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(\mathrm Z_s\) be the reductive center of \(\mathrm G_s\) is in fact purely geometric, i.e. it suffices to verify it over the algebraic closure of \(\kappa(s)\), as follows from the second assertion of 4.2.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.4

Let G be an affine algebraic group over a field \(k\). Then G admits a reductive center.

Since the center of G is representable by a closed subgroup of G (VIII 6.7), we are at once reduced to the case where G is commutative. Moreover, by virtue of 4.2 we may assume \(k\) algebraically closed. We will see in XVII that in this case G can be written as a product \(\mathrm Z \times \mathrm U\), where Z is of multiplicative type, and U "unipotent", from which it will follow at once that Z is indeed a reductive center of G. We will give here a proof independent of the structure theorem for commutative algebraic groups in the general form just indicated.
Let us note that it follows from IX 6.8 that the set of subgroups of multiplicative type H of G is filtered increasing. Let us show that it admits a largest element.

When G is smooth over \(k\), we apply the structure theorem BIBLE 4 th. 4, \[ \mathrm G = \mathrm G_s \times \mathrm G_u, \] with \(\mathrm G_s\) of multiplicative type and \(\mathrm G_u\) "unipotent", which means here that \(\mathrm G_u\) admits a composition series whose factors are subgroups (smooth if one insists) of \(\mathbb{G}_a\). (Indeed, \(\mathrm G_u/\mathrm G_u^0\) is a unipotent group by BIBLE 4, cor. to th. 3, hence a \(p\)-group where \(p\) = characteristic exponent, thanks to BIBLE 4 prop. 4; on the other hand \(\mathrm G_u^0\) admits a composition series with connected smooth quotients of dimension 1 by BIBLE 6 th. 1, cor. 1, and the latter are isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\) by BIBLE 7 th. 4). Now one sees easily (cf. lemma below) that every homomorphism from a group of multiplicative type to \(\mathbb{G}_a\), hence also to \(\mathrm G_u\), is trivial, which indeed proves that every subgroup of multiplicative type of G is contained in \(\mathrm G_s\).

In the general case, consider the subgroup \[ \mathrm G_0 = \mathrm G_{\mathrm{réd}} \] of G, which is smooth over \(k\), hence by what precedes admits a largest subgroup of multiplicative type \(\mathrm Z_0\). The subgroups of multiplicative type of G containing \(\mathrm Z_0\) correspond to subgroups of multiplicative type of \(\mathrm G' = \mathrm G/\mathrm Z_0\). Now we have an exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm G_0/\mathrm Z_0 \longrightarrow \mathrm G/\mathrm Z_0 \longrightarrow \mathrm G/\mathrm G_0 \longrightarrow 0, \] where by virtue of what precedes, \(\mathrm G_0/\mathrm Z_0\) has no subgroup of multiplicative type except the unit group. Since a subgroup of a group of multiplicative type is of multiplicative type (IX 8), it follows that for every subgroup of multiplicative type H of \(\mathrm G/\mathrm Z_0\), we have \(\mathrm H \cap (\mathrm G_0/\mathrm Z_0) = 0\), hence \(\mathrm H \to \mathrm G/\mathrm G_0\) is injective. Since \(\mathrm G/\mathrm G_0\) is a radicial algebraic group, hence finite over \(k\), this implies that H is itself radicial and of rank bounded by that of \(\mathrm G/\mathrm G_0\). This implies that the family of subgroups of multiplicative type of G admits a largest element, say Z.

I claim that \(\mathrm G/\mathrm Z\) has no subgroup of multiplicative type other than the unit subgroup. This follows from the fact (proved in 7.1.1) that a commutative extension of two algebraic groups of multiplicative type is of multiplicative type: so if \(\mathrm Z'\) is the inverse image in G of a subgroup of multiplicative type of \(\mathrm G/\mathrm Z_0\), then \(\mathrm Z'\) is of multiplicative type, hence \(\mathrm Z' = \mathrm Z\) by the maximality of Z, hence \(\mathrm Z'/\mathrm Z = 0\).

It now follows easily from the "principle of finite extension" that for every extension K of \(k\), \((\mathrm G/\mathrm Z)_{\mathrm K} = \mathrm G_{\mathrm K}/\mathrm Z_{\mathrm K}\) likewise has no subgroup of multiplicative type except the unit group.

We can now prove that Z is a reductive center of G. Indeed, let \(u : \mathrm H \to \mathrm G_{\mathrm S}\) be a homomorphism of S-groups, where S is a prescheme over \(k\) and H a group of multiplicative type and of finite type over S; let us prove that \(u\) factors through \(\mathrm Z_{\mathrm S}\). It amounts to the same to say that the composite homomorphism \(\mathrm H \to \mathrm G_{\mathrm S} \to (\mathrm G/\mathrm Z)_{\mathrm S} = \mathrm G_{\mathrm S}/\mathrm Z_{\mathrm S} = \mathrm U_{\mathrm S}\) is zero. Now I claim that, setting \(\mathrm U = \mathrm G/\mathrm Z\), every homomorphism \(u : \mathrm H \to \mathrm U_{\mathrm S}\) is zero. Indeed, by virtue of IX 5.2 we are reduced to the case where S is the spectrum of a field, and by IX 6.8 this then follows from the fact that \(\mathrm U_{\mathrm K}\) has no subgroup of multiplicative type other than 0. This completes the proof of 4.4. It only remains to supply the proof of the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.4.1

Let H be an S-group prescheme of multiplicative type; then every homomorphism of S-groups \(u : \mathrm H \to \mathbb{G}_a\) is trivial.

Indeed, consider the module \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm S}^2 = \mathrm E\) as an extension of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm S} = \mathrm E'\) by \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm S} = \mathrm E''\); then \(\mathbb{G}_a\) is identified with the prescheme of automorphisms of this extension, so a homomorphism \(u : \mathrm H \to \mathbb{G}_a\) is identified with an H-module structure on E respecting the extension structure, i.e. such that \(\mathrm E'\) is stable under H and the operations induced by H on \(\mathrm E'\) and \(\mathrm E''\) are trivial. By virtue of I 4.7.3 it follows that H operates trivially on E, hence \(u\) is trivial. Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 4.4.2

If G is a nonzero abelian variety over \(k\), then G does not admit a reductive center in the sense of 4.1 with the restriction "G affine" omitted, for, for \(n\) prime to the characteristic, \({}_n\mathrm G\) is étale over \(k\) of order prime to the characteristic, hence of multiplicative type; now the family of the \({}_n\mathrm G\) is schematically dense in G, so if there were a reductive center, it would be identical to G, which is absurd, G not being of multiplicative type. This is the reason why it is appropriate in 4.1 to impose the restriction that G have affine fibers.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.5

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth over S, affine over S, with connected fibers, T a maximal torus of G, \(u : \mathrm H \to \mathrm G\) a central homomorphism, with H an S-group prescheme of multiplicative type and of finite type. Then \(u\) factors through T.

Indeed, let C be the centralizer of T, which is a closed subgroup prescheme of G smooth over S (XI 5.3), hence affine over S. Since T is in the center of G, it is normal, and we may consider the quotient group \(\mathrm G/\mathrm T = \mathrm U\), which is representable (VIII 5.1). Since \(u\) is central, it factors through C, and everything comes down to proving that the composite homomorphism \(\mathrm H \to \mathrm C \to \mathrm U = \mathrm C/\mathrm T\) is trivial. By virtue of IX 5.2 we are reduced to the case where S is the spectrum of a field, which we may assume algebraically closed. But then by virtue of BIBLE 6, th. 2, U is a connected algebraic group (smooth over \(k\)) which is "unipotent", which means, as we have already observed, that it admits a composition series with quotients isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\). Hence every homomorphism from a group of multiplicative type of finite type H to U is trivial. This proves 4.5.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.6

Let G be as in 4.5. If G admits a reductive center, the latter is contained in every maximal torus of G.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.7

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth, affine over S, and with connected fibers.

a) For every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), let \(z(s)\) be the type of the reductive center of \(\mathrm G_s\) (which is defined by virtue of 4.4). Order the set E of classes, up to isomorphism, of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-modules of finite type, by declaring that the class of M is larger than that of \(\mathrm M'\), if \(\mathrm M'\) is isomorphic to a quotient of M. Then the map \(\mathrm S \to \mathrm E\), \(s \mapsto z(s)\) is lower semicontinuous.

b) For G to admit a reductive center Z, it is necessary and sufficient that the preceding function \(z : \mathrm S \to \mathrm E\) be locally constant. If this is so, \(\mathrm G/\mathrm Z\) is representable (cf. VIII 5.1), and \(\mathrm G/\mathrm Z\) admits the unit subgroup as reductive center.

c) Suppose that the reductive rank of G is locally constant (cf. 1.7 b). Then G admits a reductive center Z. If \(\mathrm G/\mathrm Z\) is representable (for example, G affine over S) then moreover the maximal tori T of G (resp. the Cartan subgroups C of G) and \(\mathrm T'\) (resp. \(\mathrm C'\)) of \(\mathrm G' = \mathrm G/\mathrm Z\) are in one-to-one correspondence, with \(\mathrm T' = \mathrm T/\mathrm Z\) (resp. \(\mathrm C' = \mathrm C/\mathrm Z\)) corresponding to T (resp. C), and \(\mathrm T = \varphi^{-1}(\mathrm T')\) (resp. \(\mathrm C = \varphi^{-1}(\mathrm C')\)) corresponding to \(\mathrm T'\) (resp. \(\mathrm C'\)), where \(\varphi : \mathrm G \to \mathrm G'\) is the canonical homomorphism.

d) Let T be a maximal torus of G, denote by \(\mathfrak g\) the Lie algebra of G, and consider the homomorphism \[ \theta : \mathrm T \longrightarrow \mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak g), \] induced by the adjoint representation of G (II 4). Then the kernel of \(\theta\) is a reductive center of G.

Proof. a) and b). The proof of a) and of the first assertion in b) is essentially identical to that of 1.7 a) and b), and we dispense with reproducing the reasoning here. Let us only point out that in a) one must appeal to IX 5.6 (using the fact that G has connected fibers). Let us prove the second assertion of b), i.e. that if Z is a reductive center of G, then \(\mathrm G' = \mathrm G/\mathrm Z\) admits the unit subgroup as reductive center. Let us note right away that by virtue of VIII 5.1 the quotient group \(\mathrm G/\mathrm Z\) is indeed representable; it is affine over S, of finite presentation over S (VIII 5.8) and even smooth over S (for it suffices to see this on the fibers, \(\mathrm G'\) being flat of finite presentation over S; now for the fibers we pointed out in VIB 9.2 (xii) that a quotient of an algebraic group smooth over a field \(k\) is smooth); finally, G having connected fibers, the same holds for \(\mathrm G'\). Thus \(\mathrm G'\) satisfies the same initial hypotheses as G. To see that the unit subgroup of \(\mathrm G'\) is a reductive center, we may confine ourselves if we wish to the case where S is the spectrum of a field (4.3). Let \(\mathrm Z'\) be a central subgroup of multiplicative type of \(\mathrm G'\); everything comes down to proving that \(\mathrm Z'\) is reduced to the unit group. Let \(\mathrm Z_1\) be the inverse image of \(\mathrm Z'\) in G, and consider the operations of \(\mathrm Z_1\) on G induced by inner automorphisms of G. Since Z is central, Z operates trivially, so that \(\mathrm Z_1\) operates through operations of \(\mathrm Z'\) on G. Moreover, Z being in the center of G, \(\mathrm Z_1\) and hence \(\mathrm Z'\) operates trivially on Z; furthermore, \(\mathrm Z'\) being in the center of \(\mathrm G'\), the operations of \(\mathrm Z'\) on the quotient \(\mathrm G/\mathrm Z = \mathrm G'\) are likewise trivial. Since Z is central, it follows at once that the operations of \(\mathrm Z'\) on G come from a group homomorphism \[ u : \mathrm Z' \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{S\text{-}gr}}(\mathrm G', \mathrm Z), \] via \[ r(z') \cdot g = g \cdot u(z')(\varphi(g)), \] where \(r : \mathrm Z' \to \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathrm{S\text{-}gr}}(\mathrm G)\) is the representation under consideration of \(\mathrm Z'\) by automorphisms of G, and \(\varphi : \mathrm G \to \mathrm G'\) the canonical homomorphism. Now the datum of a group homomorphism \(u\) as above amounts to the datum of a group homomorphism \[ v : \mathrm G' \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{S\text{-}gr}}(\mathrm Z', \mathrm Z), \] and on the other hand, by virtue of X 5.8, the second member is representable and is a group étale over S, so its unit section is an open immersion, hence \(\mathrm{Ker}\, v\) is an open subgroup of \(\mathrm G'\). Since \(\mathrm G'\) has connected fibers, it is equal to \(\mathrm G'\), hence \(v\) is zero, hence \(u\) is zero, hence \(\mathrm Z'\) operates trivially on G, hence the same holds for \(\mathrm Z_1\), which is therefore central in G. Thus \(\mathrm Z_1\) is a commutative extension of a group of multiplicative type \(\mathrm Z'\) by a group of multiplicative type Z, hence (as we are over a field) is a group of multiplicative type (7.1.1). Being central in G, it is contained in the reductive center Z, i.e. \(\mathrm Z_1 = \mathrm Z\), whence \(\mathrm Z' = \) the unit group, which completes the proof of b).

d) Let \(\mathrm Z = \mathrm{Ker}\,\theta\), which is a subgroup of multiplicative type of T (for example by virtue of IX 6.8). By virtue of 4.5, every central homomorphism \(u : \mathrm H \to \mathrm G\), with H of multiplicative type and of finite type, factors through Z. Since the formation of Z is compatible with any base change, it remains to prove that Z is central, i.e. that the centralizer C of Z is equal to G. Now, by virtue of XI 5.3, C is a smooth closed subgroup of G; on the other hand, since Z operates trivially on \(\mathfrak g\), one sees that \(\mathrm{Lie}(\mathrm C) = \mathfrak g\). One concludes easily that \(\mathrm C = \mathrm G\): indeed, the immersion \(\mathrm C \to \mathrm G\) is étale, for it is so fiber by fiber (as an unramified homomorphism of smooth algebraic groups of the same dimension, VIB 1.3) and we may apply X 3.5. Thus \(\mathrm C \to \mathrm G\) is a closed étale immersion, hence an open immersion (SGA 1 I 5.1), and since it is also a closed immersion and G has connected fibers, it is an isomorphism.

c) By virtue of 1.7 b), G admits, locally for the fpqc topology, a maximal torus, hence by virtue of 4.2 and of d) just proved, G admits a reductive center Z. We saw in d) that every maximal torus T of G contains Z. One verifies at once that \(\mathrm T' = \mathrm T/\mathrm Z\) is a torus; to prove that it is a maximal torus in \(\mathrm G'\), we are reduced by definition 1.3 to the case where S is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field, and then the assertion is contained in BIBLE 7 th. 3 a). Conversely, let \(\mathrm T'\) be a maximal torus of \(\mathrm G'\), and let \(\mathrm T = \varphi^{-1}(\mathrm T')\); let us prove that T is a maximal torus of G. The question being local for the fpqc topology, we may assume that G already admits a maximal torus, say \(\mathrm T_0\), so that by virtue of what precedes, \(\mathrm T'_0 = \mathrm T_0/\mathrm Z_0\) is a maximal torus of \(\mathrm G'\). By virtue of the conjugation theorem 1.7 c), \(\mathrm T'\) and \(\mathrm T'_0\) are locally conjugate for the fpqc topology, hence (\(\varphi : \mathrm G \to \mathrm G'\) being covering for this topology, so that every section of \(\mathrm G'\) lifts locally to a section of G) T and \(\mathrm T_0\) are likewise locally conjugate. Since \(\mathrm T_0\) is a maximal torus, the same therefore holds for T. One proceeds analogously for the Cartan subgroups. This completes the proof.

Let us give a useful translation of d), in the case where T is diagonalizable, hence of the form \(\mathbf{D}_{\mathrm S}(\mathrm M)\), where M is a free \(\mathbb{Z}\)-module of finite type. Then (I 4.7.3) the T-module \(\mathfrak g\) decomposes as a direct sum of sub-T-modules \(\mathfrak g_m\) (\(m \in \mathrm M\)): \[ \mathfrak g = \coprod_{m \in \mathrm M} \mathfrak g_m, \] which are necessarily locally free. Suppose that for every \(m \in \mathrm M\), the rank of \(\mathfrak g_m\) is constant (which is the case in particular if S is connected). Then the set R of those \(m \in \mathrm M\) such that \(\mathfrak g_m \neq 0\) ("roots") is finite. This granted:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.8

Under the preceding conditions and with the preceding notations, the reductive center of G is the intersection of the kernels of the root characters \(m \in \mathrm R\) on T. We thus have an isomorphism \[ \mathrm Z \simeq \mathbf{D}_{\mathrm S}(\mathrm N), \] where N is the quotient of M by the subgroup generated by R.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.9

Let G be an algebraic group over the algebraically closed field \(k\). Suppose G smooth over \(k\), connected affine, with reductive center reduced to the unit group, and that the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak g\) of G is nilpotent. Then G is "unipotent", i.e. admits a composition series with quotients isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\).

By virtue of BIBLE 6, th. 4, cor. 3 it suffices to prove that a maximal torus T of G is reduced to the unit group, or what amounts to the same, that the Lie algebra \(\mathfrak t\) of T is reduced to 0. Now it follows from the fact that the T-module \(\mathfrak g\) decomposes according to the characters \(\alpha\) of T (I 4.7.3), that for every \(t \in \mathfrak t\), the operation \(\mathrm{ad}_{\mathfrak g}(t)\) on \(\mathfrak g\) is semisimple. So if \(\mathfrak g\) is nilpotent, \(\mathrm{ad}_{\mathfrak g}(t)\) is zero. Now by virtue of 4.7 d), the reductive center of G being reduced to the identity element, the homomorphism \(\mathrm T \to \mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak g)\) is a monomorphism, hence induces an injective map on Lie algebras, which means (II 4.5) that for \(t \in \mathfrak t\), the relation \(\mathrm{ad}_{\mathfrak g}(t) = 0\) implies \(t = 0\). This proves that \(\mathfrak t = 0\) and completes the proof.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.10

Let G be an affine, smooth, connected algebraic group over an algebraically closed field \(k\). Then the reductive center of G is the intersection of the maximal tori of G.

Of course, this means the intersection in the schematic sense (or what amounts to the same, in the sense of subfunctors of G), i.e. the largest closed subprescheme of G dominated by the maximal tori of G. It follows from the noetherian character of G that it is also the intersection of a suitable finite set of maximal tori of G.

Let Z be the intersection in question; Z is a closed subgroup of a torus, hence is of multiplicative type; on the other hand, by virtue of 4.5 it contains the reductive center of G. To prove that it is equal to it, it remains to prove that it is central. Since G is connected, it suffices by virtue of IX 5.5 to prove that Z is normal. Now by construction Z is invariant under the \(\mathrm{int}(g)\), with \(g \in \mathrm G(k)\), so the normalizer N of Z (cf. VIII 6.7) is a closed subgroup of G which contains the rational points of G. Since G is reduced, we have \(\mathrm N = \mathrm G\), which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.11

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth over S, affine over S, with connected fibers, and of unipotent rank zero, which implies (1.9) that G is of locally constant reductive rank, hence (1.10) that the "prescheme of maximal tori of G" is defined, say \(\mathscr{T}\), and is smooth, separated, of finite type over S. Let G operate on \(\mathscr{T}\) via inner automorphisms, whence a homomorphism of group functors \[ u : \mathrm G \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathrm S}(\mathscr{T}). \] Under these conditions, the three following subfunctors of G are identical:

  1. (i)

    The reductive center Z of G.

  2. (ii)

    The center \(\mathrm Z'\) of G.

  3. (iii)

    The kernel \(\mathrm Z'' = \mathrm{Ker}\, u\) of the preceding homomorphism.

In particular, the center of G is representable by a subgroup of multiplicative type of G.

Proof. We trivially have \(\mathrm Z \subset \mathrm Z' \subset \mathrm Z''\); it remains to prove that \(\mathrm Z'' \subset \mathrm Z\), which comes down to proving (the hypotheses being stable under base change) that every section \(g\) of G over S which operates trivially on \(\mathscr{T}\) is a section of Z. Introducing \(\mathrm G' = \mathrm G/\mathrm Z\) and using 4.7 b) and c), which imply in particular that the prescheme \(\mathscr{T}'\) of maximal tori of \(\mathrm G'\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\mathscr{T}\) (XI 5.5 bis), we are reduced to the case where \(\mathrm G = \mathrm G'\), i.e. to the case where the reductive center Z of G is zero. (N.B. note that by virtue of 4.7 c), the unipotent rank of \(\mathrm G'\) is equal to that of G, hence is zero since that of G is.) We must therefore prove in this case that \(g\) is the unit section of G. The usual reduction procedure brings us to the case where S is noetherian, and even to the case where S is local artinian (since, to verify that \(g\) is the unit section, it suffices to verify, when S is locally noetherian, that this is so after every base change \(\mathrm S' \to \mathrm S\), with \(\mathrm S'\) local artinian). Now in this case the kernel \(\mathrm Z''\) of \(u\) is representable (VIII 6.2 a) and 6.5 c)) (note that \(\mathrm Z''\) is representable by XI 6.8). To prove that \(\mathrm Z''\) is reduced to the unit subgroup, it suffices by virtue of Nakayama to prove that this is so for its fiber \(\mathrm Z''_0\). This therefore brings us to the case where S is the spectrum of a field \(k\), which we may obviously assume algebraically closed. Now \(\mathrm Z''\) is contained in the stabilizer of every point of \(\mathscr{T}(k)\), i.e. in the normalizer \(\mathrm N(\mathrm T)\) of every maximal torus T of G. Since the reductive rank of G is zero, it follows by XI 5.9 that T is an open subgroup of \(\mathrm N(\mathrm T)\), hence that the Lie algebra of \(\mathrm N(\mathrm T)\) is identical to that of T. Hence the Lie algebra of \(\mathrm Z''\) is contained in that of T. On the other hand, it follows from 4.10 that the intersection of the Lie algebras of the maximal tori T of G is nothing other than the Lie algebra of the reductive center Z, hence here zero, since we assumed \(\mathrm Z = 0\). Consequently, the Lie algebra of \(\mathrm Z''\) is zero, i.e. \(\mathrm Z''\) is étale over \(k\). Moreover \(\mathrm Z''\) is obviously normal in G, and since G is connected it follows easily that \(\mathrm Z''\) is in the center of G. It is therefore in \(\mathrm T = \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\) for every maximal torus T, hence in the intersection of the maximal tori, i.e. in \(\mathrm Z = 0\) by 4.10, which completes the proof.

pages.title: 5. Application to the scheme of subgroups of multiplicative type7

blocks.html: 5. Application to the scheme of subgroups of multiplicative type7

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 5.1

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth and affine over S, M the "prescheme of subgroups of multiplicative type of G", representing the functor made explicit in XI 4.1. For every integer \(n > 0\), let \(\mathrm T_n\) be the subfunctor of M such that \(\mathrm T_n(\mathrm S') = \) the set of subgroup preschemes of multiplicative type H of \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm S'}\) such that \(n\, \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm H} = 0\), so that \(\mathrm T_n\) is representable, and is affine over S (XI 3.12 a)). Let \(u_n : \mathrm M \to \mathrm T_n\) be the morphism defined by \(u_n(\mathrm H) = {}_n\mathrm H\) (where \({}_n\mathrm H = \mathrm{Ker}(n\,\mathrm{id}_{\mathrm H})\)). With these notations, we have the following:

a) Every subprescheme U of M, of finite type over S, is contained in a closed subprescheme X of M, of finite type over S, and every closed subprescheme X of M, of finite type over S, is affine over S.

b) Suppose S quasi-compact, and let X be a closed subprescheme of M of finite type over S. Then there exists an integer \(n > 0\) such that for every multiple \(m\) of \(n\), the induced morphism \[ u_m|_{\mathrm X} : \mathrm X \longrightarrow \mathrm T_m \] is a closed immersion.

Proof. a) To prove the first assertion of a), we will take for X the schematic closure of U (EGA I 9.5.1 and 9.5.3), which is defined since the immersion \(i : \mathrm U \to \mathrm M\) is quasi-compact (for U is of finite type over S and M separated over S (4.1)). We must therefore prove that such an X is affine over S, which will prove at the same time the second assertion of a). In the preceding form, one sees that the question is local on S, which we may thus assume affine. Then U, being of finite type over S, is contained in a quasi-compact open subset of M, and this reduces us to the case where U is itself an open subset, of finite type over S i.e. quasi-compact. (N.B. A closed subscheme of a scheme affine over S is affine over S).

It suffices to prove that such a U is dominated by a closed subprescheme of M which is affine. In this form, the usual reduction procedure brings us at once to the case where S is noetherian. One proceeds in the same way for b), which reduces to the case where S is affine noetherian.

Let us take up again the projective limit T of the \(\mathrm T_n\) used in the proof of XI 4.1, which is a prescheme affine (but not of finite type) over S, and whose local rings are noetherian rings, as was seen at the beginning of the proof of IX 3.7. (N.B. For \(t \in \mathrm T\), \(t = (t_n)\), the transition morphisms \(\mathrm T_m \to \mathrm T_n\) being smooth and the dimension of the \(\mathrm T_m\) bounded, there exists an \(n_0\) such that the \(\mathrm T_n \to \mathrm T_{n_0}\) are étale at \(t_n\) for every \(n\) a multiple of \(n_0\).) The proof of loc. cit., or XI 3.11, shows that the canonical morphism \[ u : \mathrm M \longrightarrow \mathrm T \] is an immersion, and induces isomorphisms on the local rings (but one should be careful that \(u\) is not in general an open immersion nor a quasi-compact morphism). Let U be a quasi-compact open subset of M; we are going to prove that its closure in T is contained in M, which proves (if X denotes the schematic closure of U in M) that \(u|_{\mathrm X} : \mathrm X \to \mathrm T\) is a closed immersion, hence that X is affine, and this will prove a). Since U is noetherian, it has only a finite number of irreducible components, and every element \(t \in \mathrm T\) in the closure of U is a specialization of an element \(x \in \mathrm U\). Since the local ring of \(t\) in T is noetherian, the same holds for the local ring A of \(t\) in \(\overline x\) equipped with the induced reduced structure.

The canonical morphism of the integral noetherian local scheme \(\mathrm S' = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm A)\) into T then sends the closed point of \(\mathrm S'\) to \(t\), the generic point to \(x \in \mathrm M\), and we must show under these conditions that \(t \in \mathrm M\). After possibly replacing A by the quotient of a suitable complete local A-algebra flat over A (EGA 0III 10.3.1) by a minimal prime ideal, we may assume that A is complete with algebraically closed residue field, (and one could even reduce to the case where it is moreover a discrete valuation ring thanks to EGA II 7.1.7). We are thus reduced (making the change of notation: \(\mathrm S'\) denoted by S) to the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.2

Let S be the spectrum of an integral noetherian local ring, complete with algebraically closed residue field, G an S-group prescheme smooth and affine over S, \((\mathrm H(n))_{n>0}\) a system of subgroups of multiplicative type of G, indexed by the integers \(n>0\), \(\eta\) the generic point of S. We suppose:

  1. a)

    If \(m\) is a multiple of \(n\), we have \(\mathrm H(n) = {}_n\mathrm H(m)\).

  2. b)

    There exists a subgroup of multiplicative type \(\mathrm H_\eta\) of \(\mathrm G_\eta\) such that we have \(\mathrm H(n)_\eta = {}_n(\mathrm H_\eta)\) for every \(n>0\).

Under these conditions, there exists a subgroup H of multiplicative type of G, such that for every \(n>0\), we have \(\mathrm H(n) = {}_n\mathrm H\).

For every \(n\), let \(\mathrm C_n = \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm H(n))\), which is a closed subgroup prescheme of G, smooth over S (XI 5.3). The set of integers \(n>0\) being ordered by divisibility, the \(\mathrm C_n\) form a decreasing family of closed subpreschemes of G, and since G is noetherian, this family is stationary. Let C be the common value of the \(\mathrm C_n\) for \(n\) large. Then C satisfies the same conditions as G; moreover the \(\mathrm H(n)\) are in fact subgroups of C. Hence, after possibly replacing G by C, we may assume that the \(\mathrm H(n)\) are central subgroups of G.

So let \(s\) be the closed point of S, and \(\mathrm Z_s\) the reductive center of \(\mathrm G_s\) (defined thanks to 4.4). By virtue of the definition (4.1), \(\mathrm Z_s\) contains the \(\mathrm H(n)_s\). Since A is complete, \(\mathrm Z_s\) comes from a subgroup prescheme of multiplicative type Z of G (XI 5.8). I claim that Z contains the \(\mathrm H(n)\). Indeed, since \(\mathrm H(n)\) is central, hence commutes with Z in G, we deduce a group homomorphism \(\mathrm Z \times \mathrm H(n) \to \mathrm G\), which by IX 6.8 admits an image group which is a subgroup of multiplicative type K of G containing Z, and everything comes down to proving that \(\mathrm K = \mathrm Z\), which follows from \(\mathrm K_s = \mathrm Z_s\) and from IX 5.1 bis.

We are thus reduced to proving the analogue of 5.2, but with G replaced by a group Z of multiplicative type and of finite type over S (not necessarily smooth over S). Since A is complete with algebraically closed residue field, Z is diagonalizable (X 3.3 and 1.4), hence of the form \(\mathbf{D}_{\mathrm S}(\mathrm M)\), M a commutative group of finite type. Consequently every subgroup of multiplicative type H of Z is diagonalizable (IX 2.11 (i)), hence of the form \(\mathbf{D}_{\mathrm S}(\mathrm N)\), where N is a quotient group of M (VIII 3.2). Thus the \(\mathrm H(n)\) correspond to quotient groups \(\mathrm M(n)\) of M, and the existence of a subgroup of multiplicative type H of Z such that \(\mathrm H(n) = {}_n\mathrm H\) for every \(n\) amounts to that of a quotient group N of M such that \(\mathrm M(n) = \mathrm N/n\mathrm N\) for every \(n\). Now this comes at once from the fact that there exists a subgroup \(\mathrm H_\eta\) of \(\mathrm G_\eta\) such that \(\mathrm H(n)_\eta = {}_n(\mathrm H_\eta)\) for every \(n\). This completes the proof of 5.2 and hence of a).

b) We already know that \(u|_{\mathrm X} : \mathrm X \to \mathrm T\) is a closed immersion. It follows easily that for \(m\) large, the composite \(u_m|_{\mathrm X} : \mathrm X \to \mathrm T_m\) is also a closed immersion. Since we will not need this fact in what follows, we dispense with detailing its proof here.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.3

With the notations of 5.1, let U be a subset of M which is both open and closed, of finite type over S. Then U is affine over S for the structure induced by M, and if S is quasi-compact, there exists an integer \(n>0\) such that for every multiple \(m\) of \(n\), the induced morphism \(u_m|_{\mathrm U} : \mathrm U \to \mathrm T_m\) is an open and closed immersion, (i.e. an isomorphism onto an open and closed subset of \(\mathrm T_m\), equipped with the induced structure).

The first assertion follows from 5.1 a), the second from 5.1 b), taking into account that \(u_m : \mathrm M \to \mathrm T_m\) is smooth (XI 2.2 bis) and that a smooth, i.e. étale, immersion is an open immersion.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.4

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth and affine over S, of locally constant reductive rank (1.7 b)), \(\mathscr{T}\) the "prescheme of maximal tori of G" (1.10). Then \(\mathscr{T}\) is smooth and affine over S. If T is a maximal torus of G, \(\mathrm N(\mathrm T)\) its normalizer, then \(\mathrm G/\mathrm N(\mathrm T)\) (XI 5.3 bis) is affine over S. The same holds for \(\mathrm G/\mathrm C(\mathrm T)\) (where \(\mathrm C(\mathrm T)\) is the centralizer of T) provided that \(\mathrm W(\mathrm T) = \mathrm N(\mathrm T)/\mathrm C(\mathrm T)\) is finite over S (cf. 2.1 b)).

The second assertion is contained in the first, since by the conjugation theorem, \(\mathrm G/\mathrm N(\mathrm T)\) is isomorphic to \(\mathscr{T}\) (XI 5.5 bis). For the first assertion, one notes that by construction, \(\mathscr{T}\) is isomorphic to an open and closed subprescheme of M (for we may assume the reductive rank of G constant and equal to \(r\), and then \(\mathscr{T}\) is the subprescheme of M which corresponds to the subtori of relative dimension \(r\), i.e. the largest subprescheme of M over which the "universal subgroup of multiplicative type" \(\mathrm H \subset \mathrm G_{\mathrm M}\) is a torus of relative dimension \(r\)). We may therefore apply 5.3. Finally, for the last assertion, one notes that \(\mathrm G/\mathrm C(\mathrm T)\) is finite over \(\mathrm G/\mathrm N(\mathrm T) \simeq (\mathrm G/\mathrm C(\mathrm T))/\mathrm W(\mathrm T)\), hence is affine since \(\mathrm G/\mathrm N(\mathrm T)\) is.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.5

Let G be an algebraic group smooth and affine over a field \(k\). Then the scheme M of subgroups of multiplicative type of G is a direct sum of schemes affine over S. For every subgroup of multiplicative type H of G, if C and N denote respectively its centralizer and its normalizer, the quotients \(\mathrm G/\mathrm C\) and \(\mathrm G/\mathrm N\) are affine.

Using XI 5.1 bis, one sees that the saturation, under G operating on M, of any closed finite subset of M is open: indeed, we reduce to the case where \(k\) is algebraically closed, hence to the case of the orbit of a point \(x\) rational over \(k\), but then by loc. cit. the morphism \(g \mapsto g \cdot x\) from G to M is smooth hence open, so its image is open.

Let U be the union of the classes of the closed points of M for the equivalence relation defined by the operations of G. Then U is open and contains every closed point of M, hence by the Nullstellensatz is identical to M. Thus M is a disjoint union of open subsets, which are therefore necessarily closed, so M is the prescheme sum of preschemas \(\mathrm M_i\), each of which is an orbit under G of a closed point, hence is quasi-compact, hence of finite type. By virtue of 5.3 the \(\mathrm M_i\) are affine. If H is a subgroup of multiplicative type of G, it corresponds to a point \(x\) of M rational over \(k\), and \(\mathrm G/\mathrm N\) is identified with the orbit of \(x\) under G (XI 5.5 bis). It is therefore affine by what precedes. Since C is an open subgroup of N (XI 5.9), \(\mathrm G/\mathrm C\) is thus finite over \(\mathrm G/\mathrm N\) (for the latter is isomorphic to \((\mathrm G/\mathrm C)/(\mathrm N/\mathrm C)\)), hence affine since \(\mathrm G/\mathrm N\) is.
This proof shows at the same time:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 5.6

Under the conditions of 5.5 for G and H, the subscheme U of M "of the subgroups of multiplicative type of G which are locally conjugate to H" is an open and closed subprescheme of G.
Pictorially speaking, every subgroup of multiplicative type \(\mathrm H'\) of G which is a limit of subgroups of G conjugate to H, is itself conjugate to H.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 5.7

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth and affine over S, H an S-prescheme of multiplicative type and of finite type; set \(\mathrm M = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{S\text{-}gr}}(\mathrm H, \mathrm G)\), which by virtue of XI 4.2 is representable and is smooth over S and separated over S. One can then prove for M a result entirely analogous to 5.1, either by reducing to 5.1 by a proof analogous to that of XI 4.2, or by proceeding directly with a proof modeled on that of 5.1. One deduces from it corresponding variants of 5.3, 5.5, 5.6, which the reader will formulate.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: For a generalization of the results of the present section to the case where G is not assumed affine over S, cf. M. Raynaud: Faisceaux amples sur les schémas en groupes et les espaces homogènes, Chap. IX.

pages.title: 6. Maximal tori and Cartan subgroups of algebraic groups not necessarily affine (algebraically closed base field)
