blocks.html: 6. Maximal tori and Cartan subgroups of algebraic groups not necessarily affine (algebraically closed base field)

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.1

Let G be a connected algebraic group over a field \(k\), Z an algebraic subgroup of finite index of its center; then \(\mathrm G/\mathrm Z\) is affine.

We may assume that Z is the center of G, for a scheme finite over an affine scheme is affine. Consider the vector spaces \[ \mathrm P^n = \mathrm P^n(\mathrm G) = \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm G,e}/\mathfrak m_{\mathrm G,e}^{n+1} \qquad (n \text{ an integer} \geqslant 0), \] where \(\mathfrak m_{\mathrm G,e}\) is the maximal ideal; then G operates on the \(\mathrm P^n(\mathrm G)\) by the adjoint representation, and if \(\mathrm Z_n\) is the kernel of the corresponding homomorphism \[ \mathrm{ad}_n : \mathrm G \longrightarrow \mathrm{GL}(\mathrm P^n), \] one verifies easily (using the fact that G is connected) that Z is the intersection of the \(\mathrm Z_n\), hence (G being noetherian) equal to one of the \(\mathrm Z_n\). But \(\mathrm{ad}_n\) defines, by passage to the quotient, a monomorphism \(\mathrm G/\mathrm Z_n \to \mathrm{GL}(\mathrm P^n)\), which is therefore a closed immersion, hence \(\mathrm G/\mathrm Z_n\) is affine, and consequently \(\mathrm G/\mathrm Z\) is.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.2

Let G be an algebraic group smooth over the field \(k\), Z a central algebraic subgroup, \(\mathrm G' = \mathrm G/\mathrm Z\), \(u : \mathrm G \to \mathrm G'\) the canonical homomorphism, T a subgroup of multiplicative type in G, \(\mathrm T' = u(\mathrm T)\), C the centralizer of T in G, \(\mathrm C'\) that of \(\mathrm T'\) in \(\mathrm G'\). Then we have \[ {\mathrm C'}^0 \subset u(\mathrm C). \]

We may assume \(k\) algebraically closed. Let \[ \mathrm C_1 = (u^{-1}(\mathrm C')^0)_{\mathrm{réd}}, \] it suffices to prove that we have \(\mathrm C_1 \subset \mathrm C\), for \(u(\mathrm C_1)\) is connected of finite index in \(\mathrm C'\), hence set-theoretically equal to \({\mathrm C'}^0\), hence equal to \({\mathrm C'}^0\) since \(\mathrm C'\) and consequently \({\mathrm C'}^0\) are smooth. Consider the morphism \[ (g,t) \longmapsto gtg^{-1}t^{-1} \] from \(\mathrm G \times \mathrm G\) to G; it induces a morphism \[ \varphi : \mathrm C_1 \times \mathrm T \longrightarrow \mathrm Z_1, \qquad \text{where} \quad \mathrm Z_1 = \mathrm Z_{\mathrm{réd}}, \] for, the first member being reduced, it suffices to see that for \(g \in \mathrm C_1(k)\), \(t \in \mathrm T(k)\), we have \(gtg^{-1}t^{-1} \in \mathrm Z(k)\), which comes from the fact that \(g\) centralizes T modulo Z. One sees easily (by computing on the points rational over \(k\)) that \(\varphi(g,t)\) is additive in \(g\), and additive in \(t\), hence is "bilinear"; I claim that (\(\mathrm Z_1\) being smooth and \(\mathrm C_1\) connected) this homomorphism is identically zero, which will indeed prove that \(\mathrm C_1 \subset \mathrm C\). Using the density theorem for T, we are reduced to the case where T is finite, i.e. where there exists an integer \(n>0\) such that \(n \cdot \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm T} = 0\). Let us note that \(\varphi\) is defined by a group homomorphism \[ \mathrm C_1 \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{S\text{-}gr}}(\mathrm T, \mathrm Z_1), \] now \(\mathrm Z_1\) being commutative and smooth, the second member is representable by an algebraic group étale over \(k\), and \(\mathrm C_1\) being connected, every group homomorphism from \(\mathrm C_1\) to the latter is zero. Q.E.D.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.3

Under the preceding conditions, suppose \(\mathrm C'\) connected; then we have \[ \mathrm C' = u(\mathrm C), \qquad \mathrm C = u^{-1}(\mathrm C'). \]
Indeed, then \(\mathrm C' = {\mathrm C'}^0\); on the other hand C obviously contains Z, whence \(\mathrm C = u^{-1}(u(\mathrm C))\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.4

Let G be an algebraic group over the field \(k\), Z a central algebraic subgroup such that \(\mathrm G/\mathrm Z = \mathrm G'\) is a torus. Then G is commutative, and if \(k\) is algebraically closed, there exists a torus T in G such that \(u(\mathrm T) = \mathrm G'\), where \(u\) is the canonical homomorphism \(\mathrm G \to \mathrm G/\mathrm Z = \mathrm G'\).

We may assume \(k\) algebraically closed. Consider again the morphism \(\mathrm G \times \mathrm G \to \mathrm G\) defined by \((g,h) \mapsto ghg^{-1}h^{-1}\); then (Z being central) this morphism factors through a morphism \(\mathrm G' \times \mathrm G' \to \mathrm G\), and \(\mathrm G'\) being commutative, the latter takes its values in Z, and even in \(\mathrm Z_1 = \mathrm Z_{\mathrm{réd}}\), since \(\mathrm G' \times \mathrm G'\) is reduced. One sees as above that the morphism \(\varphi : \mathrm G' \times \mathrm G' \to \mathrm Z_{\mathrm{réd}}\) thus obtained is bilinear, hence zero, which proves that G is commutative. To find a torus T of G such that \(u(\mathrm T) = \mathrm G'\), we may (after possibly replacing G by \(\mathrm G^0_{\mathrm{réd}}\)) assume G smooth and connected, and moreover (after possibly replacing Z by \(\mathrm Z^0_{\mathrm{réd}}\)) that Z is smooth and connected. By a well-known theorem of Chevalley8, Z is an extension of an abelian variety by an affine group, which reduces us at once to proving our assertion in each of the two following cases: 1) Z is affine, 2) Z is an abelian variety. In case 1), G is affine and the result is well known (BIBLE 7 th. 3 a)). So suppose that Z is an abelian variety. Since every homomorphism from the additive group \(\mathbb{G}_a\) to the torus \(\mathrm G' = \mathrm R\) or to the abelian variety Z is trivial, it follows that the same holds for every homomorphism from \(\mathbb{G}_a\) to G, so G contains no subgroup isomorphic to \(\mathbb{G}_a\). By the structure theorem of Chevalley already invoked, we have an exact sequence \[ (\ast) \qquad 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm T \longrightarrow \mathrm G \longrightarrow \mathrm A \longrightarrow 0, \] where A is an abelian variety, and T an affine group smooth and connected. Since the latter is commutative and contains no additive subgroup, it follows that T is a torus (and it is obviously the unique maximal torus of G). Everything comes down to proving that every epimorphism \[ u : \mathrm G \longrightarrow \mathrm R, \] where R is a torus, satisfies \(u(\mathrm T) = \mathrm R\). Set \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr}}(\mathrm T, \mathbb{G}_m) = \mathrm M, \qquad \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr}}(\mathrm R, \mathbb{G}_m) = \mathrm P, \] (these are free \(\mathbb{Z}\)-modules of finite type which give back T, R up to isomorphism via \(\mathrm T = \mathbf{D}_k(\mathrm M)\), \(\mathrm R = \mathbf{D}_k(\mathrm P)\)), and let \[ \mathrm B = \mathrm{Ext}^1_{k\text{-}\mathrm{gr}}(\mathrm A, \mathbb{G}_m), \] (B is also the set of points rational over \(k\) of the dual abelian variety of A). We obviously have \[ (\mathrm{xx}) \qquad \mathrm{Ext}^1(\mathrm A, \mathrm T) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr}}(\mathrm M, \mathrm B), \qquad \mathrm{Ext}^1(\mathrm A, \mathrm R) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{gr}}(\mathrm P, \mathrm B), \] in particular the extension G of A by T is given by a homomorphism \[ \theta : \mathrm M \longrightarrow \mathrm B. \] On the other hand the exact sequence \((\ast)\) gives the exact sequence \[ 0 \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm A, \mathrm R) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm G, \mathrm R) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm T, \mathrm R) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ext}^1(\mathrm A, \mathrm R), \] moreover \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm A, \mathrm R) = 0\), and \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm T, \mathrm R) \to \mathrm{Ext}^1(\mathrm A, \mathrm R)\) is identified with the homomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm P, \mathrm M) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm P, \mathrm B) \] deduced from \(\theta : \mathrm M \to \mathrm B\). Setting \[ \mathrm N = \mathrm{Ker}(\theta), \] we thus find a canonical bijection \[ \mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm G, \mathrm R) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm P, \mathrm N) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(\mathrm S, \mathrm R), \qquad \text{where} \quad \mathrm S = \mathbf{D}_k(\mathrm N), \] which can be described geometrically in an immediate way as follows:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.5

Let G be an extension of an abelian variety A by a torus \(\mathrm T = \mathbf{D}_k(\mathrm M)\), defined by a homomorphism \(\theta : \mathrm M \to \mathrm B = \mathrm{Ext}^1(\mathrm A, \mathbb{G}_m)\) (base field \(k\) algebraically closed). Let \(\mathrm N = \mathrm{Ker}\,\theta\), \(\mathrm S = \mathbf{D}_k(\mathrm N)\) the corresponding torus, isomorphic to \(\mathrm T/\mathrm U\) where \(\mathrm U = \mathbf{D}_k(\mathrm M/\mathrm N)\), and consider the extension \(\mathrm H = \mathrm G/\mathrm U\) of A by S. This extension splits9, so we have a unique projection of H onto S, whence a unique homomorphism \[ v : \mathrm G \longrightarrow \mathrm S \] extending the canonical homomorphism \(\mathrm T \to \mathrm S\). This granted, for every torus R, and every homomorphism \(u : \mathrm G \to \mathrm R\), there exists a unique homomorphism \(u' : \mathrm S \to \mathrm R\) such that \(u = u'v\). In particular, we have \(\mathrm{Im}(u) = \mathrm{Im}(u') = u'(\mathrm T)\).
This shows in particular that if \(u\) is an epimorphism, the same holds for its restriction to T, which completes the proof of 6.4.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6.6

Let G be a smooth connected algebraic group over an algebraically closed field \(k\).

a) The maximal tori of G are conjugate.

b) Let T be a torus of G; then its centralizer is smooth and connected.

c) The map \(\mathrm T \mapsto \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\) establishes a one-to-one correspondence between the set of maximal tori T of G, and the set of Cartan subgroups (No. 1) of G. For an algebraic subgroup C of G to be a Cartan subgroup, it is necessary and sufficient that it be smooth, nilpotent and set-theoretically equal to its connected normalizer, (and then it is even equal to its connected normalizer); we then have \(\mathrm C = \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\), where T is the unique maximal torus of C, and \(\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T) = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm C)\).

d) Let \(v : \mathrm G \to \mathrm H\) be an epimorphism of smooth connected algebraic groups; then the maximal tori (resp. the Cartan subgroups) of H are the images of the maximal tori (resp. of the Cartan subgroups) of G. If T is a maximal torus of G, C its centralizer, then \(v(\mathrm C)\) is the centralizer of \(v(\mathrm T)\).

e) Under the conditions of d), suppose that \(\mathrm{Ker}\, v\) is a central subgroup of G; then \(\mathrm T \mapsto v(\mathrm T)\) (resp. \(\mathrm C \mapsto v(\mathrm C)\)) establishes a one-to-one correspondence between the set of maximal tori (resp. of Cartan subgroups) of G, and the set of maximal tori (resp. of Cartan subgroups) of H. The Cartan subgroups of G contain the center of G and a fortiori \(\mathrm{Ker}\, v\), and are the groups of the form \(v^{-1}(\mathrm C')\), where \(\mathrm C'\) is a Cartan subgroup of H.
Let us also make explicit right away the following immediate consequence of c):

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 6.7

For G to be nilpotent (i.e. the group \(\mathrm G(k)\) nilpotent) it is necessary and sufficient that the tori in G be central, or again that G have only a single maximal torus, (and then the latter is the largest subtorus of G).

Proof of 6.6. Let Z be a central algebraic subgroup of G, let \(\mathrm G' = \mathrm G/\mathrm Z\), and \(u : \mathrm G \to \mathrm G'\) the canonical homomorphism. Then \(\mathrm G'\) is a smooth connected group. If \(\mathrm T'\) is a subtorus of \(\mathrm G'\), it follows from 6.4 that \(u^{-1}(\mathrm T')\) is commutative, and that \(\mathrm T'\) is the image of a subtorus of \(u^{-1}(\mathrm T')\), hence of a subtorus of G. Since \(u^{-1}(\mathrm T')\) is commutative, it obviously admits a largest subtorus T (for the sum of two subtori gives a third one containing both), and we thus have \(u(\mathrm T) = \mathrm T'\). From this it follows immediately that for every maximal torus T of G, its image \(\mathrm T' = u(\mathrm T)\) is a maximal torus of \(\mathrm G'\), and that \(\mathrm T \mapsto u(\mathrm T)\) is a one-to-one correspondence between maximal tori of G and maximal tori of \(\mathrm G'\).

We now take \[ \mathrm Z = \mathrm{Centr}(\mathrm G)^0_{\mathrm{réd}}, \] so that \(\mathrm G'\) is affine by virtue of 6.1. Since the maximal tori of \(\mathrm G'\) are then conjugate, the same therefore holds for those of G, which proves a). Moreover, for G to be nilpotent, resp. to have only a single maximal torus, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathrm G'\) satisfy the same condition; now \(\mathrm G'\) being affine, the two conditions in question on \(\mathrm G'\) are equivalent (BIBLE 6 th. 4 cor. 2), hence the same holds for the conditions in question for G. Moreover, if G has only a single maximal torus T, the latter is normal hence central, and since every torus in G is contained in a maximal torus, it is central. Conversely, if every torus is central, the same holds for the maximal tori, and by the conjugation theorem a) there is only a single maximal torus. This proves 6.7.

Let T be an arbitrary torus of G, \(\mathrm T' = u(\mathrm T)\); then \(\mathrm C' = \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G'}(\mathrm T')\) is connected (BIBLE 6 th. 6 a)), hence by virtue of 6.3 the centralizer C of T is equal to \(u^{-1}(\mathrm C')\), hence connected (since Z is connected), which proves b). If T is maximal, then we know that \(\mathrm C'\) is nilpotent, hence C (which is a central extension of \(\mathrm C'\)) is nilpotent. Moreover, T is a maximal torus of C, hence by virtue of 6.7 it is the unique maximal torus of C; consequently the map \(\mathrm T \mapsto \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\) from the set of maximal tori of G to the set of Cartan subgroups is bijective.

Moreover we have \[ \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T) = \mathrm C \subset \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm C) \subset \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T) \] and since we know that the centralizer of T is of finite index in its normalizer (cf. XI 5.9, whose reasoning is valid without the affineness hypothesis, using only the representability of the two functors in question, as was pointed out in XI 6.5), and that C is smooth and connected, we conclude \[ \mathrm C = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm C)^0. \] Moreover, by the one-to-one correspondence between the maximal tori and the Cartan subgroups, one sees that \(\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm C)\) and \(\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\) have the same points with values in \(k\), and since the second is smooth, we have \[ \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T) = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm C). \]

To finish establishing c), it remains to prove that if C is a smooth nilpotent connected subgroup of G which is of finite index in its normalizer, then C is a Cartan subgroup. Now since Z is central, the normalizer of C contains Z, and since Z is smooth and connected, we conclude \(\mathrm Z \subset \mathrm C\), whence \(\mathrm C = u^{-1}(\mathrm C')\), where \(\mathrm C' = u(\mathrm C)\). We then have \[ \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm C) = u^{-1}(\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G'}(\mathrm C')), \] which proves that \(\mathrm C'\) is nilpotent connected of finite index in its normalizer, hence by virtue of BIBLE 7 th. 1 it is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm G'\), whence at once C is a Cartan subgroup of G.

Let us prove e): we are under the conditions of the beginning of the proof, (setting \(\mathrm{Ker}\, u = \mathrm Z\), \(\mathrm G' = \mathrm H\)); we have already seen that \(\mathrm T \mapsto u(\mathrm T)\) is a one-to-one correspondence between maximal tori of G and maximal tori of \(\mathrm G'\). Taking into account the one-to-one correspondence between maximal tori and Cartan subgroups that we have just proved, we deduce a one-to-one correspondence between Cartan subgroups C of G and Cartan subgroups \(\mathrm C'\) of \(\mathrm G'\), by making correspond to \(\mathrm C = \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\) the group \(\mathrm C' = \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G'}(\mathrm T')\), where \(\mathrm T' = u(\mathrm T)\). Since \(\mathrm C'\) is connected by virtue of b), it follows by 6.3 that \(\mathrm C' = u(\mathrm C)\), and \(\mathrm C = u^{-1}(\mathrm C')\), which proves e).

It remains to prove d). Let T be a maximal torus of G; let us prove that \(v(\mathrm T)\) is a maximal torus of H (which, taking into account the conjugation theorem a), will imply that the maximal tori of H are all of the form \(v(\mathrm T)\) as above). So let R be a torus in H containing \(v(\mathrm T)\), and let us prove \(\mathrm R = v(\mathrm T)\). After possibly replacing H by R, and G by \(v^{-1}(\mathrm R)^0_{\mathrm{réd}}\), we may assume \(\mathrm R = \mathrm H\), i.e. that H is a torus, and we are reduced to proving that then \(v(\mathrm T) = \mathrm H\). Let again \(\mathrm Z = \mathrm{Centr}(\mathrm G)^0_{\mathrm{réd}}\), \(\mathrm G' = \mathrm G/\mathrm Z\), and \(\mathrm H' = \mathrm H/v(\mathrm Z)\): \[ \text{(D)} \qquad \xymatrix{ e \ar[r] & \mathrm Z \ar[r] \ar[d]_-{v''} & \mathrm G \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{v} & \mathrm G' \ar[r] \ar[d]^-{v'} & e \\ e \ar[r] & v(\mathrm Z) \ar[r] & \mathrm H \ar[r]_-{u'} & \mathrm H' \ar[r] & e } \] We already know that \(u(\mathrm T)\) is a maximal torus of \(\mathrm G'\), and since \(\mathrm G'\) is affine, hence \(v' : \mathrm G' \to \mathrm H'\) an epimorphism of smooth connected affine groups, \(v'(u(\mathrm T))\) is a maximal torus of \(\mathrm H'\) (BIBLE 7 th. 3 a)), hence equal to \(\mathrm H'\), i.e. \(u'(v(\mathrm T)) = \mathrm H'\); so to prove \(v(\mathrm T) = \mathrm H\) it suffices to show that \(v(\mathrm T) \supset v(\mathrm Z)\). Now \(v(\mathrm Z)\) is a smooth connected subgroup of H, hence a torus, and \(v'' : \mathrm Z \to v(\mathrm Z)\) is an epimorphism, so by virtue of 6.4 we have \(v(\mathrm Z) = v''(\mathrm S)\), where S is a torus of Z. Now S, being central in G, is obviously contained in the maximal torus T, whence \(v(\mathrm Z) \subset v(\mathrm T)\). This proves assertion d) in the case of maximal tori.

Taking into account the one-to-one correspondence between maximal tori and Cartan subgroups, it remains to prove that if T is a maximal torus of G, C its centralizer, then \(v(\mathrm C)\) is the centralizer of \(v(\mathrm T)\). For this, let us take up again the diagram (D) above (where of course H is no longer assumed to be a torus); let \(\mathrm T' = u(\mathrm T)\), \(\mathrm C' = u(\mathrm C)\); we have already seen in e) that \(\mathrm C'\) is the centralizer of the maximal torus \(\mathrm T'\), hence (\(\mathrm G'\) being affine, hence \(\mathrm H'\) being affine) \(v'(\mathrm C')\) is the centralizer of the maximal torus \(v'(\mathrm T')\) of \(\mathrm H'\) (BIBLE 7 th. 3 a), i.e. \(u'(v(\mathrm C))\) is the centralizer of \(u'(v(\mathrm T))\); since C contains Z, hence \(v(\mathrm C)\) contains \(v(\mathrm Z)\), \(v(\mathrm C)\) is therefore the inverse image under \(u'\) of \(u'(v(\mathrm C))\), i.e. of the centralizer of \(u'(v(\mathrm T))\); it is therefore the centralizer of \(v(\mathrm T)\), as follows from e) applied to \(u' : \mathrm H \to \mathrm H'\) and to the maximal torus \(v(\mathrm T)\) of H. This completes the proof of 6.6.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: references to be supplied here.

pages.title: 7. Application to smooth group preschemes not necessarily affine7

blocks.html: 7. Application to smooth group preschemes not necessarily affine7

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 7.1

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth, separated and of finite type over S. We suppose that G admits, locally for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology, a maximal torus. Then:

a) The map \[ \mathrm T \longmapsto \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T) \] induces a bijection from the set of maximal tori of G to the set of Cartan subgroups of G. If C corresponds to T, then T is the unique maximal torus of C.

b) Let T, \(\mathrm T'\) be two maximal tori of G, C, \(\mathrm C'\) the corresponding Cartan subgroups; then we have \[ \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T, \mathrm T') = \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm C, \mathrm C') = \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T, \mathrm C'), \] the first two terms are also identical to the strict transporters; finally the functor in question is representable by a closed subprescheme of G smooth over S. The tori T, \(\mathrm T'\) and the Cartan subgroups C, \(\mathrm C'\) are conjugate locally for the étale topology.

c) There exists, locally for the étale topology, a maximal torus of G and a Cartan subgroup of G.

d) Suppose that every finite subset of a fiber \(\mathrm G_s\) of G is contained in an affine open subset of G (for example G quasi-projective over S, or S artinian); then the functor \(\mathscr{T} : (\mathrm{Sch})^{\circ}_{/\mathrm S} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) defined in 1.10 (functor of maximal tori of G), isomorphic to the functor \(\mathscr{T} : (\mathrm{Sch})^{\circ}_{/\mathrm S} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) of Cartan subgroups of G, is representable by a prescheme smooth, separated of finite type over S, which is quasi-projective over S when G is, and is affine over S when G is affine over S, or when S is artinian.

e) Let \(u : \mathrm G \to \mathrm G'\) be a morphism of S-group preschemes, where \(\mathrm G'\) is smooth, separated of finite type over S, and suppose that for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), we have \(u_s(\mathrm G_s) = \mathrm G'_s\) i.e. \(u_s\) is faithfully flat (Exp. VI10). Then for every maximal torus T of G, \(u(\mathrm T)\) is a maximal torus of \(\mathrm G'\); if \(\mathrm G'\) has connected fibers and C is a Cartan subgroup of G, \(u(\mathrm C)\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm G'\), and if C is the centralizer of T, \(u(\mathrm C)\) is the centralizer of \(u(\mathrm T)\). In both cases, the induced morphism \(\mathrm T \to u(\mathrm T)\), resp. \(\mathrm C \to u(\mathrm C)\), is faithfully flat.

f) Under the conditions of e), suppose moreover that \(\mathrm{Ker}\, u\) is a central subgroup of G. Then \(\mathrm T \mapsto u(\mathrm T)\) is a bijective map from the set of maximal tori of G onto the set of maximal tori of \(\mathrm G'\), and if \(\mathrm G'\) has connected fibers, \(\mathrm C \mapsto u(\mathrm C)\) is likewise a bijective map from the set of Cartan subgroups of G onto the set of Cartan subgroups of \(\mathrm G'\); if \(\mathrm C' = u(\mathrm C)\), we have \(\mathrm C = u^{-1}(\mathrm C')\).

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 7.2

We will see in XV that the conclusion of d) remains valid without the restrictive condition on the finite subsets of the \(\mathrm G_s\). We will also prove there the conclusions concerning only the Cartan subgroups contained in b) c) d) e), when one supposes only that G admits, locally for the faithfully flat quasi-compact topology, a Cartan subgroup (but not necessarily a maximal torus). For this, we will moreover have to use 7.1 in the case where S is artinian. Moreover, the proof of c) and of d) (in the case S non-artinian) simplifies considerably by using the method of XV.

Proof of 7.1. a) Proceeding as in 3.2, one sees that for every maximal torus T of G, \(\mathrm C = \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\) is indeed a Cartan subgroup of G, and T is determined in terms of C as the unique maximal torus of C, so that it only remains to show that every Cartan subgroup C of G is defined by a maximal torus of G, or again that C admits a central maximal torus. The question being again local for the fpqc topology, we may assume that G admits a maximal torus T. Then by virtue of XI 6.2, \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T, \mathrm C)\) is representable by a closed subprescheme \(\mathrm S'\) of G smooth over S, and moreover \(\mathrm S' \to \mathrm S\) is surjective, as follows from 6.6 c). Hence, after possibly making the base change \(\mathrm S' \to \mathrm S\), we may assume that there exists a section \(g\) of G such that \(\mathrm{ad}(g) \cdot \mathrm T \subset \mathrm C\), but then \(\mathrm{ad}(g) \cdot \mathrm T\) is a maximal torus of C, which is central fiber by fiber by virtue of 6.6 c), hence central by virtue of IX 5.6 b). Q.E.D.

b) Let T, \(\mathrm T'\) be two maximal tori of G, and C, \(\mathrm C'\) the corresponding Cartan subgroups of G. Then the following conditions are equivalent: (1) \(\mathrm T \subset \mathrm T'\) (2) \(\mathrm T = \mathrm T'\) (3) \(\mathrm T \subset \mathrm C'\) (4) \(\mathrm C \subset \mathrm C'\) (5) \(\mathrm C = \mathrm C'\).

This follows trivially from a). Using the same result after arbitrary base change, one concludes the identity stated in b), between the various transporters and strict transporters. Moreover, we already remarked in a) that \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T, \mathrm C')\) is representable by a closed subprescheme of G, smooth over S, and that its structural morphism is surjective. Consequently, by Hensel there exists, locally for the étale topology, a section of this prescheme over S, so T and \(\mathrm T'\) on the one hand, C and \(\mathrm C'\) on the other hand, are conjugate locally for the étale topology, which proves b).

c) Suppose first that S is artinian, local. When G admits a maximal torus T, it follows from the conjugation theorem proved in b) that the functor \(\mathscr{T}\) of maximal tori of G is representable by the homogeneous space \(\mathrm G/\mathrm N\), where N is the normalizer of T in G, which is smooth by virtue of b). Moreover, as was observed in VIA 3.2.1, since \(\mathscr{T}\) is a homogeneous space under G, every finite subset of \(\mathscr{T}\) is contained in an affine open subset. In the general case, there exists a finite extension \(k'\) of the residue field \(k\) of S such that \(\mathrm G_{k'}\) has a maximal torus; then \(k'\) comes from \(k\) by a finite flat base change \(\mathrm S' \to \mathrm S\), and the maximal torus of \(\mathrm G_{k'}\) lifts to a maximal torus of \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm S'}\) (XI 2.1 bis), so the functor \(\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm S'}\) is representable by a prescheme over \(\mathrm S'\), smooth separated and of finite type over \(\mathrm S'\), every finite subset of which is contained in an affine open subset. Hence the natural descent datum on \(\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm S'}\) is effective, so \(\mathscr{T}\) is representable, and by descent one sees that \(\mathscr{T}\) is smooth over S, separated and of finite type over S. From the smoothness it follows, thanks to Hensel, that \(\mathscr{T}\) admits a section locally for the étale topology. This proves c) and d) in the case S artinian (N.B. we will prove below that in this case, \(\mathscr{T}\) is in fact affine over S).

Suppose now S arbitrary. To prove c) and d), which are local assertions on S for the Zariski topology, we may assume that there exists a prime number \(\ell\) prime to the residue characteristics of S, that S is affine, and that the reductive rank of the fibers of G (which is obviously locally constant, thanks to the hypothesis of the local existence for fpqc of a maximal torus) is constant, say \(r\). Let \(\mathrm S' \to \mathrm S\) be a faithfully flat and quasi-compact morphism, \(\mathrm S'\) affine, such that \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm S'}\) admits a maximal torus \(\mathrm T'\). Let \(\mathrm C'\) be its centralizer; I claim that there exists a suitable power \(n\) of \(\ell\) such that we also have \(\mathrm C = \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G}({}_n\mathrm T)\). Indeed, to see this, we are reduced at once to the case \(\mathrm S'\) noetherian, where this was seen in XI 6.2. Fixing \(n\) in this way, set \[ \mathrm M = (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^r, \qquad \mathrm P = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{S\text{-}gr}}(\mathrm M_{\mathrm S}, \mathrm G), \] then P is obviously representable as a closed subprescheme of finite presentation of \(({}_n\mathrm G)^r\), where \[ {}_n\mathrm G = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{S\text{-}gr}}((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})_{\mathrm S}, \mathrm G), \] which is also the kernel of the \(n\)-th power morphism in G, hence representable by a closed subprescheme of finite presentation of G. Moreover P is smooth by virtue of XI 2.1. Let \(s \in \mathrm{S}\); by virtue of what we saw above, there exists a finite separable extension \(k''\) of \(\kappa(s)\) such that there exists a maximal torus \(\mathrm T''\) in \(\mathrm G_{k''}\); moreover, since \({}_n\mathrm T''\) is étale over \(k''\) (\(n\) being prime to the characteristic of \(k''\)) we may assume (after possibly replacing \(k''\) by a finite separable extension) that \({}_n\mathrm T''\) is isomorphic to \(\mathrm M_{k''}\). Let \(\mathrm S'' \to \mathrm S\) be an étale morphism, such that there exists an \(s'' \in \mathrm S''\) above \(s \in \mathrm S\), giving rise to the residue extension \(\kappa(s'') \simeq k''\). We thus have a section of \(\mathrm P_{\mathrm S''} \otimes \kappa(s'')\) over \(\mathrm{Spec}(\kappa(s''))\), so by Hensel, after possibly replacing \((\mathrm S'',s'')\) by \((\mathrm S''', s''')\) étale over it, we may assume that we have a section of \(\mathrm P_{\mathrm S''}\) over \(\mathrm S''\), i.e. an element of \(\mathrm P(\mathrm S'')\), which extends the given section. In other words, we thus have a homomorphism \(\mathrm M_{\mathrm S''} \to \mathrm G_{\mathrm S''}\) which induces an isomorphism \(\mathrm M_{\kappa(s'')} \simeq {}_n\mathrm T''\). By virtue of IX 6.4 (which here reduces to a simple application of Nakayama's lemma) this homomorphism is a closed immersion above an open neighborhood of \(s'\), which we may assume equal to \(\mathrm S''\). Let \(\mathrm H''\) be its image, and consider its centralizer \(\mathrm C''\) in \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm S''}\), which is a smooth closed subprescheme of G, by virtue of XI 6.2. Let us note moreover:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.3

Under the preceding conditions for \(\ell, n, r\), for every prescheme \(\mathrm S''\) over S and every maximal torus \(\mathrm T''\) of \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm S''}\), we have \[ \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G_{\mathrm S''}}(\mathrm T'') = \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G_{\mathrm S''}}({}_n\mathrm T''). \]

Indeed, by faithfully flat descent from \(\mathrm S' \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm S''\), we are reduced to the case where \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm S''}\) admits a maximal torus \(\mathrm T''_1\) for which the preceding relation is true. But since \(\mathrm T''\) is locally conjugate to \(\mathrm T''_1\) for the étale topology by virtue of b), it follows that the same relation is true for \(\mathrm T''\).

Applying the preceding result with \(\mathrm{Spec}(\kappa(s''))\) in place of \(\mathrm S''\), one sees that the fiber \(\mathrm C''_{s''}\) is a Cartan subgroup of G. Let us now note that G being smooth over S, the union of the identity components of the fibers \(\mathrm G_s\) is an open subset of G (by virtue of a general result of EGA IV on smooth morphisms11), obviously stable under the group law of G; it is therefore a subgroup \(\mathrm G^0\) of G for the structure induced by G. Moreover, \(\mathrm G^0\) satisfies the preliminary hypotheses on G, and there is an obvious one-to-one correspondence between the maximal tori of G and those of \(\mathrm G^0\). Hence to prove c) and d), we may assume G has connected fibers, which we will do. Then the Cartan subgroups of G have connected fibers (6.6 a)). This granted, I claim that \({\mathrm C''}^0\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm S''}\) above an open neighborhood of \(s''\) in \(\mathrm S''\) (which will complete the proof of c)). This indeed follows from the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.4

Under the conditions of 7.1, suppose G has connected fibers, let C be a closed subgroup prescheme of G, smooth over S, and \(s\) an element of S such that \(\mathrm C_s\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm G_s\); then \(\mathrm C^0\) is a Cartan subgroup of G above an open neighborhood of \(s\).

One easily reduces to the case where S is local and \(s\) its closed point, and to proving that then C is a Cartan subgroup of G, then by flat descent to the case where G admits a maximal torus, say T. Then by virtue of XI 6.2, \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T, \mathrm C)\) is representable by a closed subprescheme of G smooth over S. Moreover, by virtue of the hypothesis on \(\mathrm C_s\), the fiber of the transporter at \(s\) is nonempty (taking into account the conjugation theorem 6.6 a)). This brings us by faithfully flat descent to the case where this transporter admits a section over S, hence to the case where C contains a maximal torus T of G. But then T is central in \(\mathrm C^0\) by virtue of IX 5.6 a), hence \(\mathrm C^0 \subset \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\), and since this is an inclusion of group schemes smooth over S, having the same relative dimension (namely the dimension of their common fiber at \(s\)) and with connected fibers, it is an equality, which completes the proof.

d) We keep the preceding notations and hypotheses for \(\ell, n, r\), and the connectedness of the fibers of G. Let \(\mathrm Q : (\mathrm{Sch})^{\circ}_{/\mathrm S} \to (\mathrm{Ens})\) be the functor defined by \[ \mathrm Q(\mathrm S') = \text{ set of subgroups of multiplicative type of } \mathrm G_{\mathrm S'} \text{ of type equal to } (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})^r = \mathrm M \ (\text{IX 1.4}). \] Then \[ \mathrm T \longmapsto {}_n\mathrm T \] is a morphism \[ \varphi : \mathscr{T} \longrightarrow \mathrm Q, \] which is a monomorphism by virtue of 7.3. I claim that Q is representable by a prescheme separated of finite presentation over S. Indeed, as we pointed out in the proof of XI 3.12 a), we have an isomorphism \[ \mathrm Q \simeq \mathrm P'/\Gamma \] where \(\mathrm P'\) is the open and closed subprescheme of the prescheme \(\mathrm P = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{S\text{-}gr}}(\mathrm M_{\mathrm S}, \mathrm G)\) introduced in c) which corresponds to the monomorphisms \(\mathrm M_{\mathrm S'} \to \mathrm G_{\mathrm S'}\) (cf. IX 6.8), and where \(\Gamma = (\mathrm{Aut}_{\mathrm{gr}}(\mathrm M))_{\mathrm S}\). The hypothesis that every finite subset of a fiber \(\mathrm G_s\) is contained in an affine open subset of G, being stable under passage to a closed subprescheme and under cartesian products, is obviously inherited by \(({}_n\mathrm G)^r\), hence by P, hence by \(\mathrm P'\), so that Q is representable by a prescheme of finite presentation over S (cf. V)12. One sees in the same way that if G is quasi-projective (resp. affine) over S, the same holds for Q. In any case, Q is separated over S. Now we have the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.5

The preceding homomorphism \(\varphi = \mathscr{T} \to \mathrm Q\) is representable by an open immersion.

In other words, we must prove that if H is a subgroup of multiplicative type of G, of type M, then there exists an open subset U of S such that for every \(\mathrm S'\) over S, \(\mathrm H_{\mathrm S'}\) is of the form \({}_n\mathrm T_{\mathrm S'}\), for a suitable maximal torus \(\mathrm T_{\mathrm S'}\) of \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm S'}\), if and only if \(\mathrm S' \to \mathrm S\) factors through U. We may obviously assume that the nilpotent rank of the fibers of G is constant (for thanks to b), it is locally constant), say \(r'\). Let \(\mathrm C = \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm H)\), which is a closed subgroup prescheme of G smooth over S (XI 6.2). Then, after possibly replacing S by the open and closed subset of points at which C is of relative dimension \(r'\), we may assume C of relative dimension \(r'\) everywhere. One then sees at once that H is of the form \({}_n\mathrm T\), for a maximal torus T of G, if and only if \(\mathrm C^0\) admits a central maximal torus T of relative dimension \(r\) everywhere with \(\mathrm H = {}_n\mathrm T\), which gives another expression of the subfunctor U of S that we want to represent (replacing in the preceding criterion S by an \(\mathrm S'\) over S). Moreover, by flat descent, we may assume that G admits a maximal torus \(\mathrm T_1\). Let R be the subfunctor \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T_1, \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm C^0}(\mathrm C^0))\) of \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T_1, \mathrm C)\); the latter is representable by a prescheme smooth over S by virtue of XI 6.2, and the former is representable by an induced open subprescheme, as follows at once from IX 5.6 a); in particular it is smooth over S. Consequently its structural morphism to S is open, so its image is open, and after possibly replacing S by the said image (equipped with the induced structure) we may assume the structural morphism surjective. Then by virtue of 1.13 applied to \(\mathrm C^0\), \(\mathrm C^0\) admits a central maximal torus T since it admits one locally for fpqc, which will obviously be of relative dimension equal to that of \(\mathrm T_1\), i.e. \(r\). Thus, the condition to be expressed for H is the equality \(\mathrm H = {}_n\mathrm T\), which by virtue of IX 2.10 again comes down to taking a suitable open (and closed) subset of S.

Lemma 7.5 thus implies that \(\mathscr{T}\) is representable by a prescheme separated, locally of finite presentation over S, and even of finite presentation over S, as one sees by going back over the proof of 7.5 to make sure that \(\varphi\) is in fact a quasi-compact open immersion, or by reducing, by faithfully flat quasi-compact descent, to the case where G admits a maximal torus, and where \(\mathscr{T}\) is therefore isomorphic to \(\mathrm G / \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\). This last expression, or alternatively XI 2.1, shows moreover that \(\mathscr{T}\) is smooth over S. Finally, if G is quasi-projective over S, the same holds for Q and hence also for \(\mathscr{T}\). If G is affine over S, the assertion that \(\mathscr{T}\) is then affine over S is put on record, having been established in 5.4 (N.B. I do not know whether, without an affineness hypothesis on G/S, it is possible to choose \(n\) in such a way that in 7.5 the open immersion is also a closed immersion). For the assertion that \(\mathscr{T}\) is affine over S if S is artinian, we are reduced to the case where S is the spectrum of a field (EGA I 6.1.7), which we may assume algebraically closed. Then thanks to f), which will be proved below, it suffices to prove the same assertion for \(\mathrm G / \mathrm{Centr}(\mathrm G)\); now the latter is affine by 6.1, so that we are under the preceding conditions. This completes the proof of d).

e) By virtue of IX 6.8, we know that there exists a subtorus \(\mathrm T'\) of \(\mathrm G'\) such that \(u\) induces a faithfully flat morphism \(\mathrm T \to \mathrm T'\) (which characterizes \(\mathrm T'\) as the subsheaf \(u(\mathrm T)\) of \(\mathrm G'\)). Let C be the centralizer of T, \(\mathrm C'\) that of \(\mathrm T'\); let us prove that the morphism \(\mathrm C \to \mathrm C'\) is flat, and faithfully flat if \(\mathrm G'\) has connected fibers. Since C, \(\mathrm C'\) are flat of finite presentation over S, we are reduced to the case of a base field (SGA1 I 5.9), which we may obviously assume algebraically closed. Moreover we may assume G, \(\mathrm G'\) connected (after possibly replacing them by \(\mathrm G^0\) and \(\mathrm G'^0\), which does not change \(\mathrm C^0\) and \(\mathrm C'^0\)), and it then suffices to apply 6.6 d), taking a) into account.

f) Taking a) and e) into account, we may confine ourselves to proving the assertion concerning the Cartan subgroups. Now since a Cartan subgroup of G is the centralizer of a maximal torus, it contains the center of G and a fortiori \(\mathrm{Ker}\, u\), hence it is of the form \(u^{-1}(\mathrm C')\), where \(\mathrm C' = u(\mathrm C)\) is the Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm G'\) considered in e). Hence the map \(\mathrm C \mapsto u(\mathrm C)\) is injective; to show that it is bijective, it suffices to see that for every Cartan subgroup \(\mathrm C'\) of \(\mathrm G'\), \(u^{-1}(\mathrm C')\) is a Cartan subgroup of G. The question being local for fpqc, we may assume that G admits a Cartan subgroup \(\mathrm C_1\), so \(u(\mathrm C_1) = \mathrm C'_1\) is a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm G'\), hence locally conjugate to \(\mathrm C'\) for fpqc by virtue of b), and since \(u^{-1}(\mathrm C'_1) = \mathrm C_1\) is a Cartan subgroup of G, it follows that \(u^{-1}(\mathrm C')\) is a Cartan subgroup of G. Q.E.D.

One may also, to prove that \(u^{-1}(\mathrm C')\) is a Cartan subgroup, note that it is flat over S since \(u\) is (SGA1 I 5.9), which brings us by definition to the case of a base field, and one may apply 6.6 e).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.6

Under the conditions of 7.1 e), \(\mathrm G'\) admits, locally for the étale topology, a maximal torus, hence satisfies the preliminary conditions on G. If moreover \(\mathrm{Ker}\, u\) is central, then the functors \(\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm G}\), \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathrm G}\) of maximal tori of G and of Cartan subgroups of G are isomorphic to the analogous functors \(\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm G'}\), \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathrm G'}\) for \(\mathrm G'\) (hence, in the case where they are representable, they are represented by isomorphic S-preschemas).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 7.7

a) Contrary to what happens in the case where G is affine over S (it suffices, in fact, that G have affine fibers, as will be seen in XVI), it is not true that the fact that G has locally constant reductive rank implies that G admits, locally for fpqc, a maximal torus. Let for example S be the spectrum of a discrete valuation ring, \(\mathrm G_1\) and \(\mathrm G_2\) group schemes smooth separated of finite type over S, such that the generic fiber of \(\mathrm G_1\) is an elliptic curve, the special fiber a group \(\mathbb{G}_m\), and the generic fiber of \(\mathrm G_2\) a group \(\mathbb{G}_m\), the special fiber a group \(\mathbb{G}_a\), and take \(\mathrm G = \mathrm G_1 \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm G_2\). Then the two fibers of G have reductive rank 1, but one sees at once that G does not admit a maximal torus locally for fpqc. It is on the other hand very plausible that the following condition (for a group smooth separated of finite type over a prescheme S) is sufficient for the existence of a maximal torus locally for the étale topology: the reductive rank and the abelian rank of the fibers of G are locally constant functions.

b) In the proof of 7.1 (notably a)) we invoked XI 6.2 in cases where S is not assumed locally noetherian. However, in the cases considered of application of XI 6.2, the reduction to the case S noetherian affine is immediate.
Here is a variant of 7.1 b):

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.8

Let G be an S-group prescheme smooth of finite presentation with connected fibers, H a group prescheme smooth over S, \(i : \mathrm H \to \mathrm G\) a monomorphism of S-groups (making H a sub-S-group of G). Then for every Cartan subgroup13 C of G, \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm C, \mathrm H)\) is representable by a closed subprescheme of G smooth over S.

The fact that the transporter is representable by a closed subprescheme of G of finite presentation is contained in XI 6.11, taking into account that C has connected fibers since G does (6.6 b)). To show that the transporter is smooth over S, we are reduced by the standard procedure to the case where S is affine noetherian, then to the case where S is local artinian, and by descent to the case where the residue field of S is algebraically closed. But then C admits a maximal torus T, which is a maximal torus of G. We may assume that the reductive rank and the nilpotent rank of the fiber \(\mathrm H_0\) are equal to those of \(\mathrm G_0\) (otherwise the transporter would be empty), but then one sees at once (using the connectedness of C and the fact that the centralizer in H of a maximal torus of H is smooth) that we have \[ \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm C, \mathrm H) = \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T, \mathrm H) \] and since we know that the second member is smooth (XI 2.5), the same holds for the first. The preceding reasoning shows more generally part b) of the

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.9

Let G and \(i : \mathrm H \to \mathrm G\) be as in 7.8; suppose moreover that for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(\mathrm H_s\) is connected, and has the same reductive rank and the same nilpotent rank as \(\mathrm G_s\) (i.e. \(\mathrm H_{\bar s}\) contains a Cartan subgroup of \(\mathrm G_{\bar s}\)). Then we have the following:

a) \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm H)\) is representable by a closed subprescheme \(\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm H)\) of G of finite presentation over S, and the canonical monomorphism \(\mathrm H \to \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm H)\) is an open immersion; consequently \(i\) is an immersion, and we have \[ \mathrm H = \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm H)^0. \]

b) For every Cartan subgroup C of G, \(\underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm C, \mathrm H)\) is a closed subprescheme of G, smooth over S, with surjective structural morphism. If C is the centralizer of a maximal torus T of G, we have moreover \[ \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T, \mathrm H) = \underline{\mathrm{Transp}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm C, \mathrm H). \]

c) Let C be a subgroup prescheme of H. For it to be a Cartan subgroup of H, it is necessary and sufficient that it be a Cartan subgroup of G.

d) Suppose that G admits, locally for the étale topology, or for the fpqc topology, a Cartan subgroup (resp. a maximal torus); then the same holds for H.

Proof. a) The representability of \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm H)\) by a closed subprescheme \(\mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm H)\) of G of finite presentation over S is contained in XI 6.11. Since H is smooth, hence flat locally of finite presentation over S, to verify that \(\mathrm H \to \mathrm{Norm}_{\mathrm G}(\mathrm H)\) is an open immersion, we are reduced to verifying it on the fibers (VIB 2.6), which brings us to the case where S is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field \(k\). We are then reduced (Exp. VI14) to verifying that the corresponding homomorphism on the Lie algebras is an isomorphism, or what amounts to the same, that \((\mathfrak g / \mathfrak h)^{\mathrm H} = 0\), where \(\mathfrak g\) and \(\mathfrak h\) are the Lie algebras of G and of H, and the exponent H denotes the invariants under H (cf. II 5.2.3 (i)). Now H contains by hypothesis a Cartan subgroup C of G, the centralizer of the maximal torus T of G, and it therefore suffices to prove that we have \[ (\mathfrak g / \mathfrak h)^{\mathrm T} = 0, \] which follows, taking into account the complete reducibility of the representations of T (I, 4.7.3), from the analogous relation \((\mathfrak g / \mathfrak c)^{\mathrm T}\), where \(\mathfrak c = \mathrm{Lie}(\mathrm C)\). As for the latter, equivalent to \[ \mathfrak g^{\mathrm T} = \mathfrak c, \] it means that the centralizer C and the normalizer N of T have the same Lie algebra, which follows from the fact that C is an open subgroup of N (XI 5.9). This completes the proof of a).
b) As was pointed out, the proof was given in 7.8.

c) Taking into account the fact that H is a subprescheme of G, the assertion reduces trivially to the case where S is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field, in which case it follows at once from the hypothesis made on H.
d) One uses b), c) and the "Hensel lemma" XI 1.10.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.10

Let G and H be as in 7.9, and suppose that for every algebraically closed field \(k\) over S, every element of \(\mathrm G_k(k)\) which normalizes \(\mathrm H_k\) is in \(\mathrm H_k(k)\). Then H is a closed subprescheme of G, and is its own normalizer.
This follows trivially from 7.9 a). We will apply 7.10 in particular to the Borel subgroups (more generally, to the parabolic subgroups) of G.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.11

Let G, H be as in 7.9. Then H contains every subgroup prescheme Z of G, central in G and flat and of finite presentation over S.

One may as usual reduce to the case S affine noetherian, then to the case S artinian, which implies that we are under the conditions of 7.1. By virtue of 7.9 c) we may assume that H contains a Cartan subgroup C of G, and we are reduced to proving that \(\mathrm C \supset \mathrm Z\). Now since S is artinian, \(\mathrm G' = \mathrm G / \mathrm Z\) is representable by a group prescheme of finite type over S, the canonical morphism \(u : \mathrm G \to \mathrm G'\) being faithfully flat and its kernel being Z (VIA 3.2). Obviously \(\mathrm G'\) is smooth over S, and one may apply 7.1 f), which implies that C is of the form \(u^{-1}(\mathrm C')\), hence contains Z.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 7.12

Let G, \(\mathrm G'\) be two S-group preschemes smooth of finite presentation, \(u : \mathrm G \to \mathrm G'\) a faithfully flat group homomorphism (i.e. for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(u_s\) is faithfully flat); suppose \(\mathrm{Ker}\, u\) central and G with connected fibers. Then the map \[ \mathrm H' \longmapsto \mathrm H = u^{-1}(\mathrm H') \] establishes a one-to-one correspondence between subgroup preschemes \(\mathrm H'\) of \(\mathrm G'\), smooth of finite presentation over S, having the same reductive rank and the same nilpotent rank as \(\mathrm G'\) at every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), and the set of subgroup preschemes H of G, smooth of finite presentation over S, having the same reductive rank and the same nilpotent rank as G at every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\). For H to have connected fibers, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathrm H'\) do.

Let H be a subgroup prescheme of G having the properties just specified. Then by virtue of 7.10, H contains \(\mathrm Z = \mathrm{Ker}\, u\), hence by virtue of the theory of faithfully flat descent, is of the form \(u^{-1}(\mathrm H')\), where \(\mathrm H'\) is a well-determined subgroup prescheme of \(\mathrm G'\), and one verifies at once, taking 6.6 e) into account, that the latter has the properties stated above. Moreover, if H has connected fibers, the same obviously holds for \(\mathrm H' = \mathrm H / \mathrm Z\). It therefore remains to prove that if one starts from a subgroup \(\mathrm H'\) of \(\mathrm G'\) having the stated properties, then \(u^{-1}(\mathrm H') = \mathrm H\) has the same properties in G; and that if \(\mathrm H'\) has connected fibers, the same holds for H. Taking 6.6 e) into account, we are reduced to proving that H is smooth over S (resp. and has connected fibers). Now H is already flat over S as the inverse image of \(\mathrm H'\), which is flat, under the flat morphism \(u\), so we are reduced to verifying that the geometric fibers of H are smooth (resp. and connected), which brings us to the case where S is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field. Then \(\mathrm H'\) contains a Cartan subgroup \(\mathrm C'\) of \(\mathrm G'\), so H contains the inverse image C of \(\mathrm C'\), which is a Cartan subgroup of G by 6.6 e), so C is smooth and connected. Consequently 7.11 follows from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7.13

Let G, \(\mathrm G'\) be two group preschemes flat of finite presentation over S, \(u : \mathrm G \to \mathrm G'\) a group homomorphism which is faithfully flat, \(\mathrm C'\) a subgroup prescheme of \(\mathrm G'\) of finite presentation over S, such that \(\mathrm C = u^{-1}(\mathrm C')\) is smooth over S (resp. with connected fibers). Then for every subgroup prescheme \(\mathrm H'\) of \(\mathrm G'\) of finite presentation over S, containing \(\mathrm C'\), and such that \(\mathrm H'\) is smooth over S (resp. with connected fibers), its inverse image \(\mathrm H = u^{-1}(\mathrm H')\) is smooth over S (resp. with connected fibers).

As we remarked above, this statement reduces at once to the case where S is the spectrum of a field. Note then that H is a principal fiber bundle with base H/C, with group C (Exp. VIB 9); on the other hand \(\mathrm H / \mathrm C \simeq \mathrm H' / \mathrm C'\) (cf. Exp. IV), and \(\mathrm H'\) being smooth (resp. connected), the same holds for \(\mathrm H' / \mathrm C'\) and hence for H/C. Since the same holds for C by hypothesis, it follows at once that the same again holds for the bundle H. Q.E.D.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: reference not located in VIB; see M. Demazure and P. Gabriel, Groupes algébriques, I, Masson (1970), proposition II.5.1.(c).
N.D.E.: this reference should be made precise.
N.D.E.: this reference should be made precise.

N.D.E.: The proof shows that it suffices to assume that C is a smooth subgroup of G each of whose geometric fibers is the connected centralizer of a subgroup of a maximal torus of \(\mathrm G_{\bar{\mathrm S}}\).
N.D.E.: reference not located in VIB; see M. Demazure and P. Gabriel, Groupes algébriques, I, Masson (1970), corollary II.5.6.

pages.title: 8. Semisimple elements, union and intersection of the maximal tori in group schemes not necessarily affine

blocks.html: 8. Semisimple elements, union and intersection of the maximal tori in group schemes not necessarily affine
Throughout this section, G denotes a group prescheme smooth of finite presentation over S, with connected fibers.

Suppose first that S is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field. When G is affine, the notion of a semisimple element of \(\mathrm G(k)\) was defined in BIBLE 4 No. 4; one verifies at once that this notion is invariant under algebraically closed extension \(k'/k\) of the base field. Moreover, it was seen in BIBLE 6 th. 5 (c) that \(g \in \mathrm G(k)\) is semisimple if and only if it is contained in a maximal torus of G. When G is no longer assumed affine, G can be written canonically (thanks to Chevalley) as an extension of an abelian variety by a smooth connected affine algebraic group: \[ (\ast) \qquad e \longrightarrow \mathrm V \longrightarrow \mathrm G \longrightarrow \mathrm A \longrightarrow e. \] We will say that an element \(g\) of \(\mathrm G(k)\) is semisimple if it is a semisimple element of \(\mathrm V(k)\). Since the maximal tori of V are obviously identical to the maximal tori of G, it amounts to the same to say that \(g\) belongs to a maximal torus of G. This is obviously again a notion invariant under algebraically closed extension \(k'/k\) of the base field \(k\).

Suppose now that S is the spectrum of an arbitrary field \(k\), and let \(g \in \mathrm G\). Then (choosing an algebraically closed extension K of \(\kappa(g)\)) one sees that \(g\) is the image of a geometric point \(g'\) of G with values in an algebraically closed extension K of \(k\), and we will say that \(g\) is semisimple if \(g'\) is semisimple, which is independent of the particular choice of \(g'\), thanks to what was said above. If \(k'\) is an extension of \(k\), then the set of semisimple elements of \(\mathrm G_{k'}\) is the inverse image of the set \(\mathrm G^{\mathrm{ss}}\) of semisimple elements of G.

Suppose finally S arbitrary; then a point \(g \in \mathrm G\) is called semisimple if it is semisimple in its fiber \(\mathrm G_s\). If \(\mathrm S' \to \mathrm S\) is an arbitrary morphism, then \(\mathrm G^{\mathrm{ss}}_{\mathrm S'}\) is thus the inverse image of \(\mathrm G^{\mathrm{ss}}\). Suppose that the functor \(\mathscr{T}\) defined in 1.10 (functor of maximal tori) is representable by a prescheme of finite presentation over S (which is the case for example if G admits, locally for fpqc, a maximal torus, by virtue of 7.1 d), at least if G is quasi-projective over S). Consider the canonical maximal torus \(\underline{\mathrm T}\) of \(\mathrm G_{\mathscr{T}}\) ("universal maximal torus of G"), and the morphism \[ u : \underline{\mathrm T} \longrightarrow \mathrm G \] induced by the projection \(\mathrm G_{\mathscr{T}} \to \mathrm G\). Then it follows at once from the definition that \(\mathrm G^{\mathrm{ss}}\) is nothing other than the image of the preceding morphism. We will conclude from this:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.1

The set \(\mathrm G^{\mathrm{ss}}\) of semisimple elements of G is locally constructible (hence constructible if S, hence G, is quasi-compact and quasi-separated).

One reduces as usual to the case where S is affine noetherian; moreover we may assume (by the usual noetherian criterion of constructibility (EGA 0III 9.2.3)) that S is integral, and confine ourselves to proving that there exists a nonempty open subset U of S such that \(\mathrm G^{\mathrm{ss}}|_{\mathrm U}\) is constructible. Taking U small enough, and replacing it if necessary by a finite covering, we may assume that G is separated over S and contains a maximal torus T. But then by virtue of 7.1 d) the functor \(\mathscr{T}\) is representable by a prescheme of finite presentation over S, and the same therefore holds for \(\underline{\mathrm T}\), whose image in G is consequently constructible. Q.E.D.

Suppose again that \(k\) is a field, and consider the subgroup H of G generated by the preceding morphism (cf. VIB 1.2). It is a smooth subgroup scheme of G, connected since \(\underline{\mathrm T}\) is, whose formation is obviously compatible with any extension of the base field (cf. VI), that of \(\underline{\mathrm T}\) being so. When \(k\) is algebraically closed, one sees right away that H is also the algebraic subgroup of G generated by the maximal tori of G, or what amounts to the same, by the tori of G, and it is also the smallest algebraic subgroup of G which contains the semisimple elements of \(\mathrm G(k)\). (In fact, these characterizations of H remain valid as soon as \(k\) is an infinite field, thanks to the fact, proved in XIV, that the set of points of T rational over \(k\) is dense in T). Moreover, H is normal in G, for to see this, we may confine ourselves to the case where \(k\) is algebraically closed, and then (H and G being smooth over \(k\)) it suffices to verify that H is stable under the inner automorphisms \(\mathrm{int}(g)\), \(g \in \mathrm G(k)\), which is obvious. (One could even show that H is a characteristic subgroup of G, i.e. stable under \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{k\text{-gr}}(\mathrm G)\)). It then follows at once from 6.6 d) that the reductive rank of G/H is zero; more precisely, if K is a normal algebraic subgroup of G, it follows at once from the fact that for \(k\) algebraically closed, the maximal tori of G/K are the direct images of the maximal tori of G, that the reductive rank of G/K is zero if and only if K contains all the maximal tori of G (\(k\) being assumed algebraically closed), or again if and only if K contains H (\(k\) being arbitrary): hence H is the smallest normal algebraic subgroup of G such that G/H is of reductive rank zero. Another obvious characterization of H is the following: it is the smallest algebraic subgroup of G having the same reductive rank as G. Let us note finally that H is affine: indeed, to see this we may again assume \(k\) algebraically closed, and taking up again the exact sequence \((\ast)\) from the beginning of the section, one notes that the maximal tori of G are contained in V, hence the same holds for H, so V being affine, H is. Let us summarize the results obtained:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.2

Let G be a smooth connected algebraic group over the field \(k\), and let \(\bar k\) be an algebraic closure of \(k\). There exists an algebraic subgroup H of G, such that \(\mathrm H_{\bar k}\) is the algebraic subgroup of \(\mathrm G_{\bar k}\) generated by the maximal tori (or again by the tori) of \(\mathrm G_{\bar k}\). The group H is also characterized as the smallest algebraic subgroup of G having the same reductive rank as G, or the smallest normal algebraic subgroup of G such that the reductive rank of G/H is zero. It is a smooth connected normal and affine subgroup of G, whose formation commutes with any extension of the base field.
To use the characterization of H in terms of G/H, it is appropriate to make explicit the

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.3

Let G be a smooth connected algebraic group over an algebraically closed field \(k\). For G to be of reductive rank zero (i.e., with the notations of 8.2, for us to have \(\mathrm H = (e)\)), it is necessary and sufficient that G be an extension of an abelian variety by a smooth connected unipotent algebraic group (i.e. a successive extension of groups isomorphic to the additive group \(\mathbb{G}_a\)).

Indeed, thanks to the Chevalley exact sequence \((\ast)\), we are reduced to proving that the reductive rank of the smooth connected affine group V is zero if and only if V is unipotent, which is contained in BIBLE 6.4. th. 4 cor. 3. Hence, with the notations of 8.2, H is the smallest normal algebraic subgroup of G such that \((\mathrm G/\mathrm H)_{\bar k}\) is an extension of an abelian variety by a smooth connected affine unipotent algebraic group. One also concludes:

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 8.4

With the notations of 8.2, for us to have \(\mathrm G = \mathrm H\) (i.e. \(\mathrm G_{\bar k}\) generated by its maximal tori) it is necessary and sufficient that G be affine and that every homomorphism from \(\mathrm G_{\bar k}\) to the additive group be trivial.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 8.5

a) Let \(\overline{\mathrm V}\) be the largest smooth connected affine algebraic subgroup of \(\mathrm G_{\bar k}\) (so that \(\mathrm G_{\bar k}\) is an extension of an abelian variety by \(\overline{\mathrm V}\)). It is well known (Rosenlicht15), if \(k\) is not perfect, that \(\overline{\mathrm V}\) is in general not "defined over \(k\)", i.e. that there does not in general exist an algebraic subgroup V of G such that \(\mathrm V_{\bar k} = \overline{\mathrm V}\). However, when \(\overline{\mathrm V}\) is generated by its maximal tori, i.e. when \(\overline{\mathrm V}\) admits no quotient group isomorphic to the additive group \(\mathbb{G}_{a, \bar k}\), such a V exists, namely the group H of 8.2. One thus sees that in this question of rationality, as in many others (cf. for example XIV No. 6), all the troubles come from the unipotent groups, i.e. from the additive group, while it is the presence of (sufficiently many) groups of multiplicative type that ensures, on the contrary, that things work well.

b) One may also introduce, with the notations of 8.2, the inverse image \(\mathrm H'\) in G of the commutator subgroup of G/H; then \(\mathrm H'\) is the smallest normal algebraic subgroup of G such that \((\mathrm G/\mathrm H')_{\bar k}\) is a commutative extension of an abelian variety by a smooth connected affine unipotent algebraic group. \(\mathrm H'\) is again a smooth connected affine subgroup of G. Let \(\mathrm H''\) be the inverse image in G of \(p^n(\mathrm G/\mathrm H')\) for \(n\) large, \(p\) being the characteristic (assumed \(> 0\)); then one sees easily that \(\mathrm H''/\mathrm H'\) is an abelian variety, and \(\mathrm H''\) is the smallest normal algebraic subgroup of G such that \(\mathrm G/\mathrm H''\) is a commutative smooth connected affine unipotent algebraic group. This granted, one sees easily that in order that \(\overline{\mathrm V} = \mathrm H_{\bar k}\) (notations of a)), i.e. in order that every homomorphism from \(\overline{\mathrm V}\) to the additive group be trivial, it is necessary and sufficient that \(\mathrm H'' = \mathrm G\), or again that every homomorphism from \(\mathrm G_{\bar k}\) to the additive group be trivial.

To finish, we are going to generalize to smooth groups with connected fibers the notion of reductive center developed in No. 4, taking our inspiration from 4.10. Let Z be the subfunctor of G defined by \[ \mathrm Z(\mathrm S') = \text{ Set of sections } g' \text{ of } \mathrm G_{\mathrm S'} \text{ over } \mathrm S' \text{ such that for every } \mathrm S'' \text{ over } \mathrm S' \] \[ \text{and every maximal torus } \mathrm T'' \text{ of } \mathrm G_{\mathrm S''}, \text{ the inverse image } g'' \text{ of } g' \text{ under } \mathrm S'' \to \mathrm S' \text{ is a section of } \mathrm T'' \text{ over } \mathrm S''. \]

Introducing the functors \(\mathscr{T}(\mathrm S') = \) the set of maximal tori of \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm S'}\), and \(\underline{\mathrm T}(\mathrm S') = \) the set of pairs \((\mathrm T', g')\), where \(\mathrm T'\) is a maximal torus of \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm S'}\) and \(g'\) a section of \(\mathrm T'\) over \(\mathrm S'\), one sees that \(\underline{\mathrm T}\) is a subfunctor of \(\mathscr{T} \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm G = \mathscr{T}_{\mathrm G}\), and with these notations, one may also write \[ \mathrm Z = \prod_{\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm G}/\mathrm G} \underline{\mathrm T} / \mathscr{T}_{\mathrm G}. \]

Using XI 6.8, and 7.1 d), which ensures the representability of \(\underline{\mathrm T}\) by a smooth S-prescheme under certain conditions, one could conclude the representability of Z under certain conditions, which we will however obtain by a more direct route below.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 8.6

Let G be an S-group prescheme smooth of finite presentation with connected fibers. We say that G admits a reductive center if the preceding functor Z (which is obviously a subgroup of G) is representable by a group of multiplicative type. We then say that Z is the reductive center of G.

One will note that if Z is a reductive center of G, then for every base change \(\mathrm S' \to \mathrm S\), \(\mathrm Z_{\mathrm S'}\) is a reductive center of \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm S'}\); on the other hand, the existence of a reductive center is obviously a local question for the fpqc topology. As for the terminology "reductive center", let us note that Z is in any case central, for obviously Z is invariant under \[ \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathrm S}(\mathrm G) \] and a fortiori it is a normal subgroup of G, and one applies IX 5.5.
Lemma 4.5 must here be replaced by:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8.7

Let \(u : \mathrm H \to \mathrm G\) be a central homomorphism, where H is of multiplicative type and of finite type over S; suppose that for every algebraically closed field \(k\) over S, \(u_k(\mathrm H_k)\) is contained in the largest smooth connected affine subgroup of \(\mathrm G_k\). Then \(u\) factors through every maximal torus T of G (hence, \(u\) in fact factors through the subfunctor Z of G defined above).

Using an easy variant of IX 5.1 bis (where the \(=\) sign would be replaced by an inclusion sign), we are reduced to the case where S is the spectrum of a field \(k\), which we may assume algebraically closed. (Reduce to the case S affine noetherian, then artinian, then use IX 3.6). Since T is contained in the largest smooth connected affine subgroup V of G, we are then reduced to the case where \(\mathrm G = \mathrm V\), i.e. where G is affine, where the result was proved in 4.5.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 8.8

Let G be an S-group prescheme smooth of finite presentation with connected fibers.

a) If S is the spectrum of a field \(k\), then G admits a reductive center Z. When G is an extension of an abelian variety by a smooth connected affine algebraic group V (for example if \(k\) is algebraically closed), then Z is also the reductive center of V, and it is the largest central subgroup of multiplicative type of V.

b) Let Z be a subgroup prescheme of multiplicative type of G. Then Z is a reductive center of G if and only if for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(\mathrm Z_s\) is a reductive center of \(\mathrm G_s\). Then Z is the largest subgroup of multiplicative type K of G such that for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(\mathrm K_s\) is contained in the reductive center of \(\mathrm G_s\); more generally, for every homomorphism \(u : \mathrm H \to \mathrm G\), with H of multiplicative type and of finite type over S, such that for every algebraically closed field \(k\) over S, \(u_k : \mathrm H_k \to \mathrm G_k\) factors through the largest smooth connected affine subgroup of \(\mathrm G_k\), \(u\) factors through Z (and in particular, \(u\) is central).

c) If G admits, locally for the fpqc topology, a maximal torus, then G admits a reductive center.

d) Let T be a maximal torus of G. Then \(\mathrm T \cap \mathrm{Centr}(\mathrm G) = \mathrm{Ker}(\mathrm T \to \mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak g))\) and it is a reductive center of G.

e) Let Z be a reductive center of G, and suppose \(\mathrm G' = \mathrm G/\mathrm Z\) representable (for example S artinian); then \(\mathrm G'\) admits the unit subgroup as reductive center, and \(\mathrm T' \mapsto \mathrm T = u^{-1}(\mathrm T')\) establishes a one-to-one correspondence between maximal tori of \(\mathrm G'\) and maximal tori of G.

Proof. a) Suppose that S is the spectrum of a field \(k\). To prove then the existence of a reductive center, we may assume \(k\) algebraically closed, and we are consequently reduced to the case where G is an extension of an abelian variety by a smooth connected affine algebraic group V. Since for every \(\mathrm S'\) over \(k\), the maximal tori of \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm S'}\) are those of \(\mathrm V_{\mathrm S'}\) (by virtue of IX 5.2 and of the fact that over an algebraically closed field, a homomorphism from a torus to an abelian variety is trivial), it follows that the functor Z defined above in terms of G is the same as the one defined in terms of V. We are thus reduced to the case of G affine. Since \(\mathscr{T}\) is representable, and necessarily "essentially free" over \(k\) (VIII 6.1), it follows that \(\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm G}\) is essentially free over G, and since \(\underline{\mathrm T}\) is a closed subscheme of \(\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm G} = \mathrm G_{\mathscr{T}}\) (by virtue for example of VIII 5.7), it follows by virtue of VIII 6.4 that \(\mathrm Z = \prod_{\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm G}/\mathrm G} \underline{\mathrm T}/\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm G}\) is representable by a closed subscheme of G. It is therefore a subgroup scheme of G; I claim that it is of multiplicative type: indeed we may assume \(k\) algebraically closed, then G admits a maximal torus T, and by definition we will have \(\mathrm Z \subset \mathrm T\), so Z is of multiplicative type as an algebraic subgroup of a group of multiplicative type (IX.8). This proves that Z is a reductive center of G. The fact that it is the largest central subgroup of multiplicative type of V is contained in 8.7.

b) The "only if" being trivial, let us prove that if Z is a subgroup of multiplicative type of G such that for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(\mathrm Z_s\) is the reductive center of \(\mathrm G_s\), then Z is a reductive center of G. We must first prove that Z is contained in every maximal torus of G (which will thus remain true after any base change): this is an immediate consequence of 8.7. Next, we must prove that if \(g\) is a section of G over S such that for every \(\mathrm S'\) over S, and every maximal torus \(\mathrm T'\) of \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm S'}\), \(g_{\mathrm S'}\) is a section of \(\mathrm T'\), then \(g\) is a section of Z. Let us note that one could reduce, as usual (taking into account that \(\prod_{\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm G}/\mathrm G} \underline{\mathrm T}/\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm G} = \mathrm Z'\) is a sheaf for fpqc which commutes with inductive limits of rings), to the case S affine, then S noetherian, and finally S artinian. Then \(\mathscr{T}\) is representable by a prescheme smooth over S by virtue of 7.1 d), so proceeding as in a), one sees that \(\prod_{\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm G}/\mathrm G} \underline{\mathrm T}/\mathscr{T}_{\mathrm G}\) is representable by a closed subscheme \(\mathrm Z'\) of G. We have already seen that \(\mathrm Z \subset \mathrm Z'\); on the other hand, by the hypothesis on Z we have \(\mathrm Z_k = \mathrm Z'_k\) (where \(k\) is the residue field); now Z being flat over S, it follows that \(\mathrm Z = \mathrm Z'\), which proves that Z is a reductive center of G. The other assertions of b) are contained in 8.7.
c) Reduces immediately to d).

d) Of course, \(\mathfrak g\) denotes the Lie algebra of G, and \(\mathrm T \to \mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak g)\) the homomorphism induced by the adjoint representation of G. We trivially have \[ \mathrm T \cap \mathrm{Centr}_{\mathrm G} \subset \mathrm{Ker}(\mathrm T \to \mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak g)), \] the proof of the reverse inclusion is the same as in 4.7 d); we do not repeat it here. Let Z be the group in question; as \(\mathrm{Ker}(\mathrm T \to \mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak g))\) it is of multiplicative type (cf. for example IX 6.8). To prove that it is a reductive center of G, we are reduced by b) to the case where S is the spectrum of a field. Let then \(\mathrm Z_0\) be the reductive center (which exists by virtue of a)); we obviously have \(\mathrm Z_0 \subset \mathrm T \cap \mathrm{Centr}(\mathrm G) = \mathrm Z\); on the other hand, since Z is a central subgroup of multiplicative type contained in the smooth connected affine subgroup T, it follows from a) that \(\mathrm Z_0 \supset \mathrm Z\), hence \(\mathrm Z_0 = \mathrm Z\). Q.E.D.

e) Under the conditions of 7.1 f), set \(\mathrm Z = \mathrm{Ker}\, u\) and suppose that for every algebraically closed field \(k\) over S, \(\mathrm Z_k\) is contained in a maximal torus of \(\mathrm G_k\). Then one verifies easily that the map \(\mathrm T' \mapsto u^{-1}(\mathrm T')\) induces a one-to-one correspondence between the set of maximal tori of \(\mathrm G'\), and the set of maximal tori of G. Applying this to the situation of 8.8 e), the desired conclusion follows at once.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 8.9

a) The proof given of 8.8 is independent of the results of No. 4, and in particular that of 8.8 a) does not use 4.4 (whose proof is somewhat laborious).

b) One sees easily that the subgroup Z of G considered in 8.6 is always central (whether it is representable or not), and it may be tempting to call it the reductive center of G in all cases.

c) One can also generalize 4.9; one finds the following statement: Let G be a smooth connected algebraic group over an algebraically closed field; for G to be an extension of an abelian variety by a smooth connected unipotent algebraic group (i.e. for the reductive rank of G to be zero, cf. 8.3), it is necessary and sufficient that the reductive center of G be reduced to the unit group, and that the Lie algebra of G be nilpotent.

footnotes.html: N.D.E.: references should be given here.

pages.title: 9. Complement: action of a group scheme and fixed points

blocks.html: 9. Complement: action of a group scheme and fixed points
The purpose of this section, added in January 2008, is to study the fixed points under the action of a group scheme.
9.1. Representability of the fixed-point functor.

Let S be a scheme, G an S-group scheme which operates on an S-scheme X. One defines the subfunctor \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}\) of X of the fixed points of X under G: for every S-scheme T, \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}(\mathrm T)\) is the subset of \(\mathrm X(\mathrm T)\) consisting of the points fixed under G (VIII.6.e).

Let us recall the notion of an "S-pure scheme" introduced in ([G-R], § 3.3, [R]). Let G be a flat S-group scheme of finite presentation. Since the fibers of G have no embedded components, the notion of purity for G takes the following form. The scheme G is S-pure if for every strictly henselian local S-scheme T, with closed point \(t\), every generic point \(\tilde x\) of a fiber of \(\mathrm G \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm T\) specializes to a point of \(\mathrm G_t\), i.e. the closure of \(\tilde x\) in \(\mathrm G \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm T\) meets \(\mathrm G_t\).
Examples.

1. (1)

    If G is quasi-finite and separated over S, G is S-pure if and only if G is finite over S.

  2. (2)

    If \(\mathrm S = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm R)\) and \(\mathrm G = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm A)\), G is S-pure if and only if A is a projective R-module ([G-R], 3.3.5). In particular G is S-pure if G is diagonalizable.

  3. (3)

    A group scheme of multiplicative type is S-pure, for the notion of purity is local for the étale topology on S.

  4. (4)

    The scheme G is S-pure if G is S-proper or if the fibers of G are irreducible or if S is semi-local artinian.

  5. (5)

    In particular, a reductive S-group scheme G is S-pure.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 9.2

Let G be an S-group scheme of finite presentation, S-flat and S-pure, which operates on an S-scheme X of finite presentation. Then the functor \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}\) of fixed points of X under G is representable by a closed subscheme of X, of finite presentation over S.

Indeed, let \(a\) be in \(\mathrm X(\mathrm S)\) and let H be the subgroup scheme of G fixing \(a\). Then \(a\) is fixed under G if and only if \(\mathrm H = \mathrm G\). Now the subfunctor C of S of coincidences (precisely, if T is an S-scheme, \(\mathrm C(\mathrm T) = \{\varnothing\}\) if \(\mathrm H \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm T \to \mathrm G \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm T\) is bijective, and \(\mathrm C(\mathrm T) = \varnothing\) otherwise) of H with G is representable by a closed subscheme of S, defined by a sheaf of ideals of finite type ([G-R], 4.1.1). One applies this result with \(\mathrm S = \mathrm X\), taking for \(a\) the universal point of X (i.e. the identity in \(\mathrm X(\mathrm X) = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm S}(\mathrm X, \mathrm X)\)).
9.3. Infinitesimal obstruction.

Under the hypothesis that G is a flat S-group scheme of finite presentation which operates (on the left) on a smooth S-prescheme X, we are now going to study the formal smoothness of the functor \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}\).

We suppose here that S is equipped with a closed subscheme \(\mathrm S_0\) defined by a quasi-coherent sheaf of ideals \(\mathscr{I}\) of square zero. We write \(j : \mathrm S_0 \to \mathrm S\), \(\mathrm G_0 = \mathrm G \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm S_0\), \(\mathrm X_0 = \mathrm X \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm S_0\).

Let \(\varepsilon_0\) be a point of \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}(\mathrm S_0)\) which lifts to a point of \(\mathrm X(\mathrm S)\). We are going to study the obstruction to lifting \(\varepsilon_0\) to a point of \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}(\mathrm S)\). Since X is smooth, one knows that the liftings of \(\varepsilon_0\) to \(\mathrm X(\mathrm S)\) form a trivial principal homogeneous space under the abelian group \[ \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm S_0}}(\varepsilon_0^*(\Omega^1_{\mathrm X_0/\mathrm S_0}), \mathscr{I}) \] (III.0.2, 0.3). One then sets \[ \mathrm L_0 = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm S_0}}(\varepsilon_0^*(\Omega^1_{\mathrm X_0/\mathrm S_0}), \mathscr{I}), \] which is an \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm S_0}\)-module. Moreover, seeing that \(\varepsilon_0\) is fixed under G, \(\mathrm L_0\) is naturally a \(\mathrm G_0\)-\(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm S_0}\)-module. We denote by \(\rho_0 : \mathrm G_0 \to \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathrm S_0}(\mathrm L_0)\) (resp. \(\rho : \mathrm G \to \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathrm S}(j_* \mathrm L_0)\)) the associated representation.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.4

There exists a certain class \(c(\varepsilon_0) \in \mathrm H^1(\mathrm G, j_* \mathrm L_0) \cong \mathrm H^1(\mathrm G_0, \mathrm L_0)\), canonically defined by \(\varepsilon_0\), such that \(\varepsilon_0\) lifts to \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}(\mathrm S)\) if and only if \(c(\varepsilon_0) = 0\).
