The adjunction \(\mathrm H^1(\mathrm G, j_* \mathrm L_0) \cong \mathrm H^1(\mathrm G_0, \mathrm L_0)\) is that of lemma III.1.1.2. We are going to work with the small fppf site on S. For every scheme T flat and of finite presentation over S, we write \(\mathrm G_{\mathrm T} = \mathrm G \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm T\), \(\mathrm X_{\mathrm T} = \mathrm X \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm T\) for the corresponding objects over T. Consider the sheaf \(\mathscr{A}\) on the small fppf site of S such that, for every T flat and of finite presentation over S, we have: \[ \mathscr{A}(\mathrm T) = \{\text{ set of liftings of } \varepsilon_0 \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm T \text{ to } \mathrm X(\mathrm T) \}. \] Seeing that the formation of \(\mathrm L_0\) commutes with flat base changes, the preceding considerations show that \(\mathscr{A}(\mathrm T)\) is a trivial principal homogeneous space under the abelian group \(\mathrm H^0(\mathrm T, j_* \mathrm L_0)\). Again from the fact that \(\varepsilon_0\) is fixed, for every T flat and of finite presentation over S, and every \(g\) in \(\mathrm G(\mathrm T)\), \(g\) acts by affine automorphisms on \(\mathscr{A}(\mathrm T)\), compatibly with the action of G on \(j_* \mathrm L_0\). That is: \[ g(a_{\mathrm T} + v) = g(a_{\mathrm T}) + \rho(g)(v), \quad v \in \mathrm H^1(\mathrm T, j_* \mathrm L_0). \] Since G is flat of finite type, one may apply these considerations by taking \(\mathrm T = \mathrm G\) and for \(g\) the universal point \(\mathrm{id}_{\mathrm G}\) of G. One thus obtains an action of the fppf sheaf G on \(\mathscr{A}\).

Let \(a\) be a lifting of \(\varepsilon_0\) in \(\mathrm X(\mathrm S)\). We denote by \(g^{\sharp}\) the universal point of G and define \(v^{\sharp} \in \mathrm H^0(\mathrm G, j_* \mathrm L_0)\) by \[ \rho(g^{\sharp})(v^{\sharp}) = g^{\sharp} . a_{\mathrm G} - a_{\mathrm G}. \] For every S-prescheme Y, we set \(z(\mathrm Y) : \mathrm G(\mathrm Y) \to \mathrm H^0(\mathrm Y, j_* \mathrm L_0)\), \(g \mapsto v^{\sharp}(g)\). This defines the 1-cocycle \(z\) in \(\mathrm Z^1(\mathrm G, \mathrm L)\) (exposé I, § 5).

Its class \(c(\varepsilon_0)\) in \(\mathrm H^1(\mathrm G, j_* \mathrm L_0)\) does not depend on the choice of \(a\). In particular, if \(\varepsilon_0\) lifts to \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}(\mathrm S)\), we have \(c(\varepsilon_0) = 0\). Conversely, if \(c(\varepsilon_0) = 0\), then there exists \(w \in \mathrm H^0(\mathrm S, j_* \mathrm L_0)\) such that \(z(\mathrm Y)(g) = g.w_{\mathrm Y} - w_{\mathrm Y}\) for every S-prescheme Y and every \(g \in \mathrm G(\mathrm Y)\). Applying this to \(\mathrm Y = \mathrm G\) and to \(g^{\sharp}\), one concludes that \(a - w \in \mathrm X^{\mathrm G}(\mathrm S)\). We have thus established that \(\varepsilon_0\) lifts to \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}(\mathrm S)\) if and only if \(c(\varepsilon_0) = 0\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 9.5

In the case where G is affine and flat of finite type over S affine, one can refine this obstruction into a class \(\tilde c(\varepsilon_0) \in \mathrm H^1_{\mathrm G_0}(\mathrm X_0, \mathrm L_0)\) where \(\mathrm H^1_{\mathrm G_0}(\mathrm X_0, \mathrm L_0)\) is the \(\mathrm G_0\)-equivariant cohomology group defined by Wevers ([W], app. C). In this case, it is not necessary to assume that \(\varepsilon_0\) lifts to \(\mathrm X(\mathrm S)\).
9.6. Smoothness of the fixed points.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 9.7

Let G be a flat S-group scheme, of finite type over a noetherian base S, which operates on a smooth S-scheme X. We suppose that G is S-pure, and that for every geometric point \(s\) over S (with algebraically closed residue field), we have \[ \mathrm H^1\bigl(\mathrm G_s, (\Omega^1_{\mathrm X_s})^*\bigr) = 0. \] Then the functor \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}\) of fixed points of X under G is representable by a closed subscheme of X smooth over S.

Let us prove theorem 9.7. We know that \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}\) is representable by a closed subscheme of X of finite presentation over S. We may assume \(\mathrm S = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm A)\) local. Following the smoothness criterion (SGA1 III.3.1.iii.bis), it suffices to verify the formal smoothness of \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}\) for \(\mathrm S' = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm A')\) and the closed subscheme \(\mathrm S'_0 = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm A'/\mathscr{I}') = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm A'')\), where \(\mathrm A'\) is a local artinian ring and \(\mathscr{I}'\) an ideal of square zero of \(\mathrm A'\). By dévissage, we reduce to the case where \(\mathscr{I}'\) is annihilated by the maximal ideal of \(\mathrm A'\). In particular, if \(k\) denotes the residue field of \(\mathrm A'\), \(\mathscr{I}'\) is a \(k\)-vector space.

We are thus given an element \(\varepsilon'_0 \in \mathrm X^{\mathrm G}(\mathrm S'_0)\) which we wish to lift to \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}(\mathrm S')\). We then write \(\mathrm X' = \mathrm X \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm S'\), \(\mathrm G' = \mathrm G \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm S'\), \(\mathrm X'_0 = \mathrm X \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm S'_0\), \(\mathrm G'_0 = \mathrm G \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm S'_0\). Seeing that \(\mathrm S'\) is affine and \(\mathrm X'\) is smooth over \(\mathrm S'\), \(\varepsilon'_0 \in \mathrm X^{\mathrm G}(\mathrm S'_0)\) lifts to \(\mathrm X(\mathrm S')\). By lemma 9.4, the class \[ c(\varepsilon'_0) \in \mathrm H^1(\mathrm G'_0, \mathrm L'_0) \] is the obstruction to lifting \(\varepsilon'_0\) to \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}(\mathrm S')\), where \(\mathrm L'_0 = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm S'_0}}(\varepsilon_0'^*(\Omega^1_{\mathrm X'_0/\mathrm S'_0}), \mathscr{I}')\). Seeing that \(\mathrm L'_0\) is a \(k\)-vector space, we have a canonical isomorphism \(\mathrm H^1(\mathrm G'_0, \mathrm L'_0) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm H^1(\mathrm G'_0 \times_{\mathrm A''} k, \mathrm L'_0)\). It suffices to show that \(\mathrm H^1(\mathrm G'_0 \times_{\mathrm A''} k, \mathrm L'_0) = 0\). Denoting by \(\bar k\) an algebraic closure of \(k\), we have an isomorphism (IX.3.1 in the affine case, lemma 9.11 in general) \[ \mathrm H^1(\mathrm G'_0 \times_{\mathrm A''} k, \mathrm L'_0) \otimes_k \bar k \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm H^1(\mathrm G'_0 \times_{\mathrm A''} \bar k, \mathrm L'_0 \otimes_k \bar k). \] One then remarks that the representation \(\mathrm L'_0 \otimes_k \bar k\) is a direct sum of copies of \((\Omega^1_{\mathrm X'_0 \times_k \bar k})^*\). By hypothesis we have \(\mathrm H^1(\mathrm G'_0 \times_{\mathrm A''} \bar k, (\Omega^1_{\mathrm X'_0 \times_k \bar k})^*) = 0\), which implies \(\mathrm H^1(\mathrm G'_0 \times_{\mathrm A''} k, \mathrm L'_0) = 0\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 9.8

Let G be a flat S-group scheme of finite presentation which operates on a smooth S-scheme X of finite presentation. We suppose that G admits a composition series whose factors are of one of the following types:

  1. (1)

    abelian S-scheme (i.e. G is smooth over S and its fibers are abelian varieties),

  2. (2)

    S-group scheme of multiplicative type,

  3. (3)

    finite étale S-group scheme, of degree invertible on S,

  4. (4)

    reductive S-group if S is a \(\mathbb{Q}\)-scheme.

Then the functor \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}\) of fixed points of X under G is representable by a closed subscheme of X, of finite presentation, smooth over S.

Indeed, if \(1 \to \mathrm G' \to \mathrm G \to \mathrm G'' \to 1\) is an exact sequence of S-groups satisfying the hypotheses of the theorem, the S-group \(\mathrm G''\) operates on the functor \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G'}\) and the functor \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G}\) is nothing other than that of the fixed points of \(\mathrm X^{\mathrm G'}\) under \(\mathrm G''\). Thus, we are reduced to the case of an S-group of one of the four types above. By passage to the limit, we may assume S noetherian. We are going to verify the cohomological criterion stated above for a fiber \(\mathrm G_s\) over a geometric point \(s\) of S and a finite-dimensional linear representation V of \(\mathrm G_s\).
In the case of an abelian scheme, every map from \(\mathrm G_s^n\) to V is constant, and consequently the \(\mathrm H^i(\mathrm G_s, \mathrm V)\) are zero for \(i > 0\).
In the case of a group scheme of multiplicative type, \(\mathrm G_s\) is a diagonalizable group. Theorem I.5.3.3 shows that the \(\mathrm H^i(\mathrm G_s, \mathrm V)\) are zero for \(i > 0\).

In the two remaining cases, the argument is the same since it rests on the semisimplicity of the linear representations of \(\mathrm G_s\). Indeed, in the case of a finite étale S-group scheme of degree invertible on S, \(\mathrm G_s\) is a finite constant group of degree invertible in the residue field \(\kappa(s)\); it is well known that every linear representation of \(\mathrm G_s\) is semisimple.

For the reductive case, the residue field \(\kappa(s)\) is assumed (algebraically closed) of characteristic zero. One knows then that every linear representation of \(\mathrm G_s\) is semisimple (see [T-Y], theorem 27.3.3).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 9.9

This applies in particular to the case of an action of G/S on a smooth S-group scheme H. If G is a group of multiplicative type acting by inner transformations on H smooth and affine over S, one then recovers corollary XI.5.3 stating the smoothness of the centralizer of G in H.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 9.10

In the case where \(\mathrm S = \mathrm{Spec}(k)\) and G is a linearly reductive algebraic group defined over the algebraically closed field \(k\), this result is due independently to Fogarty ([F], theorem 5.4) and Iversen ([I], proposition 1.3).
The proof of theorem 9.7 uses the following lemma, well known in the case of an affine group scheme (IX.3.1).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9.11

Let G be a group scheme over an affine scheme \(\mathrm S = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm A)\), and \(f : \mathrm S' = \mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm A') \to \mathrm S\) a flat morphism. Set \(\mathrm G' = \mathrm G \times_{\mathrm S} \mathrm S'\). Then for every quasi-coherent G-\(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm S}\)-module M, we have canonical isomorphisms \[ \mathrm H^i(\mathrm G, \mathrm M) \otimes_{\mathrm A} \mathrm A' \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm H^i(\mathrm G', \mathrm M \otimes_{\mathrm A} \mathrm A') \qquad (i \geqslant 0). \]

Seeing that the chains of degree \(n \geqslant 1\) for Hochschild cohomology are determined by their value at the point \((\mathrm{id}, \ldots, \mathrm{id}) \in \mathrm G(\mathrm G) \times \cdots \mathrm G(\mathrm G)\), we have an isomorphism of A-modules \(\mathrm C^n(\mathrm G, \mathrm M) \cong \Gamma(\mathrm G^n, \mathrm M \otimes_{\mathrm A} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm G^n})\). The cohomology group \(\mathrm H^n(\mathrm G, \mathrm M)\) is the \(n\)-th cohomology group of the complex \[ \mathrm L \longrightarrow \Gamma(\mathrm G, \mathrm M \otimes_{\mathrm A} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm G}) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\mathrm G^2, \mathrm M \otimes_{\mathrm A} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm G^2}) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\mathrm G^3, \mathrm M \otimes_{\mathrm A} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm G^3}) \longrightarrow \cdots \] Since \(\mathrm A'\) is flat over A, we have, by EGA IV1, 1.7.21, \[ \Gamma(\mathrm G^n, \mathrm M \otimes_{\mathrm A} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm G^n}) \otimes_{\mathrm A} \mathrm A' \xrightarrow{\sim} \Gamma(\mathrm G'^n, (\mathrm M \otimes_{\mathrm A} \mathrm A') \otimes_{\mathrm A'} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm G'^n}). \] This implies the desired assertion by taking cohomology.
